stem: A 35-year-old construction worker is brought to the emergency department after falling approximately 10 feet from scaffolding and landing on his feet with his right leg extended and abducted. He immediately felt severe pain in the groin and hip and was unable to walk or bear weight. He denies head trauma or loss of consciousness. He has no prior orthopedic history and is otherwise healthy. No medications or substance use are reported. On physical examination, he is alert and hemodynamically stable. The right lower extremity appears shortened and is held in a position of abduction and external rotation at the hip. There is no swelling or deformity of the thigh. Distal pulses are intact and equal bilaterally. Sensation and motor strength are preserved distally in the affected limb. There is tenderness over the anterior hip, and passive movement of the joint elicits pain. Radiographs are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Anterior hip dislocation presents with a shortened, externally rotated, and abducted leg after trauma. It occurs less frequently than posterior dislocation but should be suspected after extension-abduction injuries.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with a shortened, externally rotated, and abducted limb following a fall with the leg extended—hallmarks of an anterior hip dislocation. Although less common than posterior dislocations, anterior dislocations typically occur when force is applied to an extended, externally rotated, and abducted leg, as in this case when the patient landed awkwardly from height. The femoral head is forced anteriorly out of the acetabulum and often ends up inferior to the pubic bone. stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling off a bunk bed and striking his head on the hardwood floor from a height of approximately 6 feet. His mother reports that he briefly lost consciousness but awoke within a few minutes and has since been drowsy and minimally responsive. On examination, his Glasgow Coma Scale score is 10. He has bilateral periorbital ecchymoses and retroauricular bruising behind the left ear. Clear fluid is observed draining from his left nostril and right ear. His vital signs are: temperature 36.9°C (98.4°F), blood pressure 152/96 mm Hg, heart rate 48/min, and respiratory rate 8/min. Noncontrast CT of the head is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Basilar skull fractures often present with Battle sign, raccoon eyes, and CSF rhinorrhea or otorrhea. Associated Cushing reflex (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) suggests increased ICP. Diagnosis is largely clinical, but CT is confirmatory.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with multiple classic findings associated with a basilar skull fracture, a serious traumatic injury involving the base of the skull. The presence of Battle sign (postauricular ecchymosis) and raccoon eyes (periorbital ecchymosis) strongly suggests fractures involving the petrous temporal bone and anterior cranial fossa, respectively. In addition, the clear fluid draining from the ear and nose is most consistent with cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) otorrhea and rhinorrhea, indicating dural tear and communication between the subarachnoid space and the external environment. stem: A 22-year-old man comes to clinic for a routine check-up before traveling abroad. He has no complaints. He is a healthy college student who recently began competing in local strongman events. For the past several months, he has followed a structured training program focused on short, high-intensity sets involving maximal effort lifting, with exercises such as weighted sled pushes, deadlifts, and farmer’s carries. He performs very little aerobic activity and does not participate in endurance sports. On physical exam, he is normotensive, with a muscular build and no abnormalities noted. His BMI is 26.1 kg/m². He enrolls in a study examining muscle adaptations in athletes. A quadriceps biopsy is taken and examined. Compared to sedentary controls, his sample shows adaptation consistent with resistance training. Which of the following best describes the expected histologic finding in this athlete?
key_teaching_point: Skeletal muscle hypertrophy is the primary adaptation to anaerobic resistance training. Fiber number remains constant; growth occurs via increased cross-sectional area of existing fibers. Aerobic training (not shown here) leads to increased mitochondria, capillaries, and oxidative enzymes. 4.1 A.Deep peroneal nerve B.Femoral nerve C.Lateral plantar nerve D.Superficial peroneal nerve E.Sural nerve AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Lower limb nerves)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: This healthy young man is engaged in high-intensity anaerobic training, as seen in strongman competitions. His routine emphasizes maximal strength and power over short durations, involving fast, explosive movements like sled pushes and deadlifts. This training style relies on type II (fast-twitch) muscle fibers, which are adapted for brief, powerful contractions but fatigue rapidly. These fibers do not favor aerobic metabolism and are not targeted in endurance-based activity. stem: A 62-year-old man presents to the primary care clinic for evaluation of a chronic wound on the bottom of his right foot. He first noticed it approximately three weeks ago when his wife pointed out blood on his sock, although he himself had not felt any pain. He reports that for the past few years, he’s had a “tingly” or “numb” feeling in both feet, especially at night when lying in bed. Occasionally, this sensation disrupts his sleep. He denies fever, chills, or drainage from the wound. The lesion does not interfere with his ability to walk, but he mentions that over the last few months, he has had difficulty feeling the gas pedal while driving. He has no back pain or radiating leg symptoms. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus for 18 years, hypertension, dyslipidemia, coronary artery disease, and peripheral vascular disease. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history but quit five years ago. On further questioning, the patient notes that he is not consistent with checking his feet and has not seen a podiatrist in years. He wears the same pair of shoes daily and admits they are quite worn. His current medications include metformin, insulin glargine, lisinopril, atorvastatin, and aspirin. On review of systems, he denies visual changes, recent falls, bowel or bladder incontinence, and upper extremity numbness. He has not noticed any weight loss or fatigue. His A1c at the last visit was 8.9%, and he has a history of non-healing wounds in the past. He has never had lower extremity amputations. On physical examination, he is afebrile and in no acute distress. There is a 2-cm shallow ulcer with a clean base and surrounding callus on the plantar surface beneath the first metatarsal head of the right foot. There is no erythema, swelling, or fluctuance. A probe-to-bone test is negative. Monofilament testing shows loss of protective sensation over the plantar surface of both feet, including the toes and heels. There is reduced vibration sense at the great toe bilaterally. Capillary refill is delayed, and dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are diminished on the right. The ankle- brachial index is 0.62 on the affected side. The patient is scheduled for foot imaging and vascular consultation. Which of the following nerves is primarily responsible for sensation to the affected area?
key_teaching_point: The lateral plantar nerve, a branch of the tibial nerve, innervates the lateral plantar surface of the foot and is commonly involved in neuropathic ulcers in diabetic patients. Loss of protective sensation in this region predisposes to painless pressure ulcers, particularly under the metatarsal heads. 5.1 A.Abductor digiti minimi B.Extensor carpi radialis longus C.Extensor carpi ulnaris D.Extensor digitorum E.Extensor pollicis longus
explanation_summary: The lateral plantar nerve is one of the two terminal branches of the tibial nerve, which itself is a branch of the sciatic nerve. The tibial nerve travels through the tarsal tunnel, posterior to the medial malleolus, where it divides into the medial and lateral plantar nerves. The lateral plantar nerve innervates the lateral portion of the sole of the foot, including the lateral toes, lateral forefoot, and much of the ball of the foot near the fifth metatarsal and lateral half of the first metatarsal. stem: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic after injuring his right wrist while playing soccer during lunch break. He recalls running backwards while defending a teammate, tripping over another player's foot, and falling awkwardly onto his outstretched right hand. Since the fall, he has complained of persistent pain at the base of his thumb, especially when twisting a doorknob or picking up objects. His parents report no visible swelling or bruising, but the boy has avoided using his right hand since the injury. On physical exam, his fingers move well and there’s no visible deformity. However, palpation over the anatomical snuffbox on the dorsal radial wrist elicits marked tenderness. Passive radial deviation also causes discomfort. A plain wrist X-ray is obtained and shows no fracture. Strain of which of the following tendons might also be seen in this patient?
key_teaching_point: The anatomical snuffbox is bordered by three tendons: abductor pollicis longus, extensor pollicis brevis, and extensor pollicis longus. The extensor pollicis longus tendon forms the medial border and is most susceptible to strain in FOOSH injuries. Even in the absence of fracture, EPL strain should be considered in patients with anatomical snuffbox tenderness.
explanation_summary: This 12-year-old boy presents with anatomical snuffbox tenderness following a fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH), a mechanism classically associated with scaphoid injury. Even when the X-ray appears normal, the presence of snuffbox tenderness is highly predictive of either a scaphoid fracture or soft tissue injury in that region. The anatomical snuffbox is a triangular depression bordered laterally by the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis, and medially by the extensor pollicis longus (EPL) tendon. stem: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic for a routine examination. She hasno current joint pain but reports occasional stiffness in her knees after sittingfor extended periods. She works from home and does not engage in regularphysical activity. Her BMI is 41 kg/m². She has no history of autoimmunedisease, prior joint trauma, or occupational joint overuse. She has beenpostmenopausal for 2 years. Vital signs and physical exam are unremarkable.Which of the following is the strongest risk factor for developing osteoarthritisin this patient?
key_teaching_point: Obesity promotes osteoarthritis through both increased mechanical stress and chronic inflammation mediated by adipokines and cytokines. It is the strongest modifiable risk factor and should be prioritized in both prevention and management strategies. 7.1 A.Anterior talofibular ligament B.Calcaneofibular ligament C.Deltoid ligament D.Fibularis longus tendon E.Posterior tibial tendon AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNoneTopic 8.1 A.Avoiding alcohol consumptionB.Decreasing body weightC.Decreasing smokingD.Increasing physical activityE.Initiating hydroxychloroquine therapyF.Starting methotrexate therapy RiskfactorsSubjectMusculoskeletal (Arthritis andspondyloarthropathies)SystemNoneTopic 9.1 A.Direct inguinal hernia B.Epigastric hernia C.Incisional hernia D.Spigelian hernia E.Umbilical hernia AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Structural disorders)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: Obesity is the most potent modifiable risk factor for the development and progression of osteoarthritis (OA), particularly in weight-bearing joints such as the knees, hips, and lumbar spine. The mechanical overload caused by excess body weight accelerates the breakdown of articular cartilage, leading to joint spacenarrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophyte formation. Each 5-unit increase in BMI has been associated with up to a 35% increased risk of knee OA. Moreover, obesity-related OA is not confined to weight-bearing joints; it is also seen in the hands, suggesting a systemic component

stem: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department after injuring his right ankle during a recreational soccer match. While attempting to block a shot, he planted his right foot and twisted awkwardly, experiencing a sudden and forceful eversion of the ankle. He immediately felt severe pain and was unable to bear weight. On physical examination, the right ankle is swollen and bruised medially. Palpation along the medial malleolus elicits marked tenderness, and passive eversion of the foot reproduces the pain. The lateral aspect is non-tender, and the anterior drawer test is negative. Neurovascular status is intact. An x-ray of the right ankle is obtained and shown below. Which of the following structures is most likely injured?
key_teaching_point: The deltoid ligament stabilizes the medial ankle and is injured with forced eversion. Medial tenderness and increased medial joint space on x-ray are classic findings. Always consider mechanism (eversion vs inversion) and localize tenderness to guide diagnosis.
explanation_summary: This young man experienced a forcible eversion of the ankle while planting his foot, which is the classic mechanism of injury for deltoid ligament rupture, the major medial stabilizer of the ankle joint. The deltoid ligament originates from the medial malleolus and inserts into the navicular, talus, and calcaneus bones, resisting eversion of the foot. Clinical signs include medial ankle swelling, tenderness along the medial malleolus, and pain with eversion. These features are all present in this case. The x-ray further supports the diagnosis. stem: A 58-year-old woman comes to the clinic for follow-up of joint symptoms.Over the past few years, she has experienced intermittent pain and swelling inher hands, wrists, and knees. These symptoms tend to worsen in the morningsand improve slightly throughout the day. She has had several similar flares inthe past that lasted a few weeks and resolved with over-the-countermedications. The patient reports increased fatigue and stiffness recently,especially in the mornings. She works part-time as a librarian and has difficultyshelving books during flares. Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetesmellitus and hypertension. She has a BMI of 32.1 kg/m² and smokes one packof cigarettes per day. She drinks one glass of wine nightly and is not on anydisease-modifying medications. Temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressureis 142/90 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physicalexamination reveals tenderness and mild swelling in both wrists and thesecond and third metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studiesreveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptideantibody, with mild elevation in ESR and CRP. Which of the followinginterventions would most likely decrease this patient’s risk of recurrentdisease flares?
key_teaching_point: Smoking is a modifiable risk factor that increases the risk of rheumatoid arthritis flares and reduces treatment efficacy. Smoking cessation should be a key part of RA management. Effective RA control includes both pharmacologic and lifestyle interventions.
explanation_summary: Smoking is one of the most strongly established environmental risk factors for both the development and progression of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), particularly in genetically predisposed individuals. It is thought to enhance citrullination of proteins, stimulating autoantibody formation (e.g., anti-CCP), and promoting chronic synovial inflammation. In patients with existing RA, smoking is associated with increased disease activity, more frequent flares, and reduced response to therapy. stem: A 70-year-old man comes to the outpatient clinic because of a bulge on the lower left side of his abdomen. He first noticed the swelling a few months ago while getting out of bed, and since then, it has gradually become more noticeable, particularly when standing or straining. He denies any trauma, prior abdominal surgery, or heavy lifting. He reports no abdominal pain, changes in bowel habits, nausea, or weight loss. His medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis. On physical examination, there is a soft, reducible mass in the left lower quadrant of the abdominal wall, located several centimeters lateral to the lateral border of the rectus abdominis. The swelling increases with Valsalva maneuver and reduces with gentle pressure. There is no overlying erythema, tenderness, or signs of bowel obstruction. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Spigelian hernias occur lateral to the rectus abdominis at the linea semilunaris, most often in older adults. The medial border of the hernia is the rectus abdominis, which is the highest-yield anatomic point to remember. 10.1 A.Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis B.Carpal tunnel syndrome C.Cervical foraminal stenosis D.Cervical spondylotic myelopathy E.Multiple sclerosis AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Arthritis andspondyloarthropathies)SystemNoneTopic 11.1 A.Extensor digitorum B.Extensor indicis C.Flexor digitorum profundus D.Flexor digitorum superficialis E.Palmaris longus AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNoneTopic 12.1 A.Distal third of the humeral shaft B.Midshaft of the radius C.Proximal third of the ulna D.Supracondylar region of the humerus E.Surgical neck of the humerus AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: This patient presents with a reducible bulge in the lower lateral abdominal wall, lateral to the rectus abdominis, which is characteristic of a Spigelian hernia. Spigelian hernias are rare, comprising less than 2% of all abdominal wall hernias, but they are tested on USMLE. The hernia occurs at the semilunar line (linea semilunaris), where the aponeurosis of the transversus abdominis muscle transitions to become the lateral border of the rectus sheath. The most high-yield anatomical detail is that the medial border of the hernia is the rectus abdominis. These hernias tend to occur in older adu

stem: A 68-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to difficulty using her hands for the past several months. She reports struggling to grasp small objects such as keys and has recently needed assistance with tasks like buttoning her blouse and opening jars. She denies any recent trauma, falls, or fever. Her symptoms have been slowly progressive and worse on the right. She also describes intermittent tingling and dull aching pain that radiates down her right arm, sometimes reaching the hand. She does not report neck stiffness, gait instability, or bowel/bladder incontinence. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both controlled with medication. Neurologic examination shows decreased grip strength and reduced fine motor coordination on the right. Reflexes are symmetric, and no spasticity or hyperreflexia is observed. There is patchy sensory loss in the right hand. What is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Cervical foraminal stenosis is caused by nerve root compression at the neural foramina due to degenerative changes. Presents with unilateral weakness, sensory loss, and pain in a dermatomal/radicular distribution without signs of cord compression. MRI of the cervical spine is the diagnostic test of choice to visualize foraminal narrowing and nerve root impingement.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with progressive weakness of hand muscles, impaired fine motor function, and radicular arm pain, particularly affecting distal function like grasping and manipulation. These findings point toward involvement of a lower cervical spinal nerve root that innervates the hand, specifically implicating a lesion at the level of the neural foramen. The symptoms are asymmetric, gradual in onset, and associated with sensory changes without signs of upper motor neuron involvement, suggesting a peripheral nerve root pathology rather than central spinal cord disease. stem: A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with difficulty moving her right index finger after an accident at home earlier in the morning. She had been rearranging storage boxes on a high shelf in her garage when a plastic container unexpectedly fell. She reflexively reached out with her right hand to catch it, but the corner of the box struck the tip of her outstretched index finger with force. She felt a sharp pain immediately and has since been unable to use the fingertip normally. She denies numbness, tingling, or pain in the other fingers. On examination, the right hand appears normal with no swelling or bruising. There is no tenderness along the metacarpals or carpal bones. The index finger has full passive range of motion, and she can extend it fully. However, when asked to actively bend the fingertip of the index finger, she is unable to flex the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint, while proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal motion remains intact. The rest of the hand functions normally. Which of the following tendons is most likely injured in this patient?
key_teaching_point: FDP is the only tendon that flexes the DIP joint; loss of DIP flexion points to FDP injury. FDS flexes the PIP joint; if PIP flexion is intact, FDS is preserved. This case demonstrates classic tendon anatomy tested on USMLE, with presentation and localization guiding the diagnosis.
explanation_summary: This 42-year-old woman presents with isolated loss of flexion at the distal interphalangeal joint of the index finger following direct trauma to the fingertip, which occurred when a falling box struck the finger in extension. This presentation is highly suggestive of injury to the flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) tendon, the only tendon responsible for flexion at the DIP joint of the fingers. The mechanism — sudden extension force applied to a flexed fingertip — is classic for a jersey finger-type injury, even though this case is not sports-related. stem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling from a tree onto his outstretched right hand. He reports severe pain in the elbow and forearm, along with swelling that developed rapidly after the fall. He has difficulty moving his fingers due to pain. There is no obvious deformity of the forearm or hand, but the elbow appears swollen and tender. Passive extension of the fingers worsens his forearm pain. Radial pulse on the injured side is faint compared to the opposite arm. The fingers are warm but capillary refill is delayed. Sensory testing shows intact sensation over the thenar eminence and dorsal thumb. Radiographs of the elbow are ordered and demonstrate a displaced fracture. Injury to which of the following anatomic locations is most likely responsible for this patient’s vascular compromise?
key_teaching_point: A supracondylar fracture of the humerus, most often from a fall on an outstretched hand in children, can injure the brachial artery and median nerve as they pass anterior to the distal humerus. Vascular injury leads to diminished radial pulse, prolonged capillary refill, and risk of forearm compartment syndrome. 13.1 A.Breech presentation at birth B.High BMI C.History of asthma treated with corticosteroids D.Learning disability E.Sedentary lifestyle AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNoneTopic 14.1 A.Achilles tendinopathy B.Calcaneal stress fracture C.Plantar fasciitis D.Retrocalcaneal bursitis E.Tarsal tunnel syndrome AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Miscellaneous)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: This patient has findings consistent with a supracondylar fracture of the humerus, a common pediatric elbow injury that often occurs after a fall onto an outstretched hand (FOOSH). The mechanism transmits force up the forearm to the distal humerus, where the thin bone between the medial and lateral columns is prone to fracture just above the condyles. In children aged 5–10, the supracondylar region is the weakest point of the distal humerus. The most important complication of supracondylar fractures is neurovascular injury, especially to the brachial artery and median nerve. The brachial arter

stem: A 13-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to persistent right knee pain that has progressively worsened over the past month. He says the pain began after he tripped during basketball practice, but there was no fall or direct trauma. Since then, he has been avoiding sports and complains of intermittent pain that radiates to the thigh. His mother reports that he has been less active recently and appears to be limping, especially at the end of the day. Past medical history is notable for childhood asthma, managed with inhaled corticosteroids, and a learning disability for which he attends special classes. He was born at term by cesarean section due to breech presentation. His diet consists largely of processed foods, and he spends several hours daily playing video games. On examination, his BMI is at the 98th percentile. He walks with an antalgic gait, and there is limited internal rotation of the right hip. Knee examination is unremarkable, and there is no joint effusion or erythema. Passive flexion of the hip elicits discomfort. Neurologic exam is normal. Which of the following is the most significant risk factor for this patient’s condition?
key_teaching_point: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) typically affects obese adolescent boys and presents with progressive hip or referred knee pain, limited hip motion, and an antalgic gait. The most significant risk factor is a high body mass index (BMI), which increases shear stress across the growth plate. Diagnosis is confirmed by imaging, and the definitive management is urgent surgical stabilization with in situ pinning to prevent further slippage and complications such as avascular necrosis.
explanation_summary: This adolescent presents with symptoms consistent with slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE), a disorder in which the femoral head slips posteriorly and inferiorly through the growth plate (physis), typically during the rapid growth of early adolescence. Common presenting features include a subacute or chronic limp, dull pain in the hip, groin, or knee, and decreased internal rotation of the hip. On physical examination, flexing the hip often causes obligate external rotation, and patients may exhibit an antalgic or waddling gait. These findings, particularly in an overweight male in the 10

stem: A 47-year-old woman comes to the outpatient clinic due to pain in her right heel that has been bothering her for the past two months. She says the pain is most noticeable when she first gets out of bed in the morning. It feels like a sharp pulling sensation along the bottom of her heel, which sometimes radiates forward along the arch of her foot. After walking for a few minutes, the pain lessens, and she is generally able to go about her day with only mild discomfort. However, the pain often returns if she has been sitting or inactive for long periods and then stands up again. She has not had any recent trauma or systemic symptoms. She works in retail and spends most of her day standing or walking on hard floors. Her BMI is 31 kg/m². On physical examination, there is point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tuberosity of the right foot. There is no swelling, erythema, or warmth. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Plantar fasciitis causes heel pain that is worst with the first steps in the morning and improves with continued walking. Exam shows point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tuberosity with no inflammation or neurologic findings. explanation_summary: This patient presents with heel pain that is worst when first getting out of bed in the morning, which is the classic presentation of plantar fasciitis, a degenerative condition involving the origin of the plantar fascia on the calcaneus. The sharp pain described on first steps after rest and improvement with continued walking is nearly pathognomonic and HY on USMLE. The pain also tends to recur after prolonged periods of sitting or inactivity, a hallmark of this condition. She has no systemic symptoms, trauma, or visible inflammation, which helps rule out other causes. Plantar fasciitis typic

stem: A 13-year-old boy presents to the clinic with a painful limp that has worsened over the past three weeks. He reports pain localized to his left groin and knee, worsened with activity. His BMI is 23 kg/m², and he is otherwise healthy with no history of trauma. Physical examination reveals limited internal rotation and abduction of the left hip. When standing, his left leg appears externally rotated and shorter than the right. Passive range of motion elicits discomfort at the left hip. His x-ray findings are shown below. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: SCFE typically affects adolescents aged 10–13 years and presents with hip or referred knee pain, limp, decreased internal rotation, and external rotation deformity.X-ray shows posterior and inferior displacement of the femoral head (“ice cream slipping off the cone”), and treatment is surgical pinning.
explanation_summary: SCFE results from displacement of the femoral epiphysis relative to the femoral neck at the growth plate and typically affects preadolescent children aged 10–13 years. The condition is more common in boys and often presents as progressive hip, groin, or knee pain with a limp. Physical exam findings include decreased internal rotation and abduction, leg shortening, and external rotation. X-ray typically shows posterior and inferior displacement of the femoral head on the neck (“ice cream slipping off the cone” appearance). stem: A 67-year-old man comes to the clinic because of gradually worsening discomfort in both shoulders and elbows over the past 2 years. The pain is worse in the evenings after his work as a handyman and improves somewhat with rest. He reports mild stiffness in the morning that lasts only a few minutes before improving. He denies fevers, rash, weight loss, or recent infections. There is no history of joint trauma or inflammatory arthritis. His medical history includes controlled hypertension. Examination shows crepitus with passive movement of the shoulders and elbows, without warmth or significant swelling. There is mild limitation in the range of motion due to discomfort. Strength is preserved, and sensation is intact. No nodules or skin lesions are present. Laboratory studies, including ESR and CRP, are within normal limits. The involved joint is most likely to show which of the following?
key_teaching_point: Osteoarthritis is a wear-and-tear joint disease with cartilage loss, subchondral bone changes, and osteophyte formation. It causes activity-related pain with minimal morning stiffness and normal labs. explanation_summary: This patient’s presentation of chronic, progressively worsening joint pain involving large joints with brief morning stiffness, crepitus, and normal inflammatory markers is consistent with osteoarthritis (OA). In OA, the degenerative process leads to progressive loss of articular cartilage, eventually exposing the subarticular (subchondral) bone plate. In response to altered joint mechanics and increased stress, there is compensatory hyperactive bone formation, leading to sclerosis and osteophyte development, visible radiographically. stem: A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressively worsening bilateral knee pain that began over three years ago. She describes the pain as dull and aching, most noticeable after walking or prolonged standing. She denies morning stiffness, systemic symptoms, or joint redness. Her past medical history includes obesity and venous stasis. On exam, she has crepitus with passive knee motion and visible bony enlargement of the distal interphalangeal joints. No erythema or warmth is present. Her BMI is 42 kg/m². Synovial fluid aspirated from the right knee is clear with a WBC count of 950/μL and normal glucose levels. A plain radiograph shows asymmetric joint space narrowing and evidence of subchondral sclerosis. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology of this patient's condition?
key_teaching_point: Osteoarthritis is a non-inflammatory degenerative joint disease caused by chondrocyte-driven cartilage erosion. The hallmark finding is subchondral eburnation, with joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and sclerosis. Synovial fluid in OA is non-inflammatory (WBC < 2,000/μL), helping distinguish it from RA, gout, or septic arthritis.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with classic findings of osteoarthritis (OA) — a degenerative joint disease characterized by progressive cartilage erosion due to mechanical stress and aging. The knee pain is chronic and activity-related, in the setting of high BMI, and physical examination shows bony hypertrophy of the DIPs, a classic OA feature. The synovial fluid is non-inflammatory with <1,000 WBC/μL, further supporting OA over an inflammatory process. X-ray findings of asymmetric joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis are also pathognomonic. In OA, the initial insult is repetitive joint use

stem: A 23-year-old man comes to the clinic with left knee pain that started during a recreational soccer match two days ago. While attempting to block a shot, another player struck the outside of his planted left knee, causing it to buckle inward. He immediately felt pain in the knee and had to stop playing. Since then, the pain has persisted, especially with turning or lateral movement, but he can bear weight. On physical exam, there is diffuse mild swelling and tenderness of the knee. When inward pressure is applied to the lateral side of the knee while stabilizing the medial ankle, the joint opens more medially than the opposite side. There is no visible deformity, joint locking, or gross instability. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: MCL injury occurs due to valgus stress (lateral-to-medial force) that stretches or tears the medial stabilizing ligament. Physical exam shows excessive medial gapping of the knee under valgus pressure compared to the unaffected side. explanation_summary: This patient presents with medial knee pain after sustaining a lateral blow to the knee, resulting in inward buckling — the classic mechanism of medial collateral ligament (MCL) injury. The MCL stabilizes the medial aspect of the knee joint and resists valgus stress. When compromised, pressure applied from lateral to medial causes the knee to open excessively along the medial side, especially compared to the contralateral leg. stem: A 27-year-old man presents to the clinic after noticing a bump on the back of his right wrist that he first saw two weeks ago. He works in IT support and spends most of the day typing or using a mouse. The lesion has gradually increased in size, but he denies any associated pain, numbness, weakness, redness, or warmth. He is concerned it may be a tumor. He has no prior history of joint pain, trauma, or systemic illness. He has not tried any medications or therapies. On physical examination, a 2-cm round swelling is observed on the dorsal aspect of the right wrist, as shown in the image below. The mass is soft, non-tender, and mobile under the skin. There is no discoloration or warmth over the area. Wrist range of motion is full and symmetric, and grip strength is preserved. No skin lesions or other joint abnormalities are seen. The patient has no constitutional symptoms. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Ganglion cysts are the most common soft-tissue masses of the wrist and hand, usually located on the dorsal surface. They are painless, mobile, and often resolve spontaneously, requiring no treatment unless symptomatic.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with a classic dorsal wrist mass that is painless, round, mobile, and slowly enlarging. These features, along with the absence of constitutional or inflammatory signs and the lesion's location on the dorsum of the wrist, are highly suggestive of a ganglion cyst. These cysts arise from herniation of joint connective tissue, resulting in a gelatinous collection of synovial fluid. stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after falling off a motorcycle at high speed. He was wearing a helmet and did not lose consciousness. On examination, he has multiple abrasions and a deep laceration over the posterior mid-arm. He is placed in a sling and reports difficulty extending his wrist. Physical exam reveals wrist and finger extension weakness as well as diminished sensation over the dorsum of the right hand and posterior forearm. Elbow flexion and shoulder abduction are preserved. Reflexes are normal. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
key_teaching_point: Injury to the radial nerve along the mid-humerus causes wrist drop and sensory loss over the posterior arm and dorsum of the hand. The nerve’s location in the spiral groove makes it especially vulnerable in midshaft humeral trauma.
explanation_summary: Radial nerve injury is a classic complication of midshaft humeral fractures or deep lacerations to the posterior arm. The radial nerve traverses the radial (spiral) groove of the humerus and is responsible for innervating the extensor muscles of the wrist and fingers, as well as the skin over the posterior forearm and dorsum of the hand. Damage at this level causes wrist drop and impaired finger extension, along with sensory loss over the radial distribution. stem: A 58-year-old man comes to the neurology clinic with several months of worsening weakness in his dominant right hand. He works as a carpenter and has noticed increasing difficulty using his tools, particularly when trying to hold small nails between his fingers or when stabilizing pieces of wood that require hand dexterity. He frequently drops screws or washers, especially when trying to pick them up with his ring and little fingers. Over time, he has also observed a slow but progressive shrinkage of the muscles between his fingers and around the base of his little finger. He initially thought it was due to aging and overuse, but now it is beginning to interfere with his work and daily functioning. He denies pain, trauma, or systemic symptoms but does report intermittent numbness and tingling in the medial hand that worsens with prolonged elbow flexion or when resting his arm on a hard surface. He has a past medical history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension. He does not smoke and drinks socially. He sleeps with his arms bent under his pillow, and he sometimes notices hand numbness in the morning that resolves after a few minutes. At work, he frequently leans his elbows against counters and tool benches. He has tried wearing a wrist splint at night without significant benefit. He has not seen any change in his grip strength while shaking hands or squeezing with his palm but finds tasks requiring finger spreading or pinching between fingers increasingly difficult. He also notes trouble playing guitar, especially forming chords that require abducting and adducting individual fingers. He has no symptoms in his left hand and no known history of neck problems. On physical exam, there is marked wasting of the hypothenar eminence and the first dorsal interosseous muscle on the right. The hand shows mild clawing of the fourth and fifth digits at rest. Strength testing reveals weakness in finger abduction and adduction, most noticeable when asked to spread his fingers against resistance or hold a piece of paper between the fingers. Sensation is reduced over the palmar and dorsal surfaces of the fifth digit and the medial half of the fourth digit. Grip strength is mildly reduced but wrist flexion, elbow strength, and forearm pronation/supination are intact. Tinel sign is positive at the right elbow. The physician explains that the pattern of sensory and motor findings indicates damage to a specific nerve supplying most of the intrinsic hand muscles. Which of the following nerves is primarily responsible for this patient’s deficits?
key_teaching_point: The ulnar nerve innervates the dorsal and palmar interossei, which control finger abduction and adduction, along with other intrinsic hand muscles. Damage at the elbow or wrist leads to motor deficits, interosseous wasting, clawing of the 4th and 5th digits, and sensory loss over the medial hand. 22.1 A.Air travelB.Family history of malignancyC.History of deep vein thrombosisD.ObesityE.Sedentary lifestyleF.Use of compression stockings PathophysiologySubjectPulmonary & Critical Care (DVT/PE) SystemNone 23.1 A.Anterior cruciate ligament tear B.Iliotibial band syndrome C.Lateral collateral ligament injury D.Lateral meniscus tear E.Medial collateral ligament injury AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNoneTopic

In this patient, the inability to spread the fingers or hold paper between them—known as Froment’s sign when the adductor pollicis is involved—is a hallmark of ulnar neuropathy, particularly involving the deep motor branch. Additionally, atrophy of the first dorsal interosseous muscle and hypothenar wasting further localize the lesion to the ulnar nerve. stem: A 48-year-old man comes to the emergency department with a swollen,painful left leg. He says this happened once before two years ago and wastold he had a “blood clot.” He recently returned from a 9-hour internationalflight and has gained about 25 lbs over the past year. He works a desk joband spends most of his day sitting. He also takes a daily selective serotoninreuptake inhibitor for depression and has a BMI of 33 kg/m². Vital signs are:temperature 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure 128/84 mmHg, heart rate 92/min,respiratory rate 16/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Onexamination, his left leg is warm, erythematous, and tender to palpation.Duplex ultrasonography confirms the presence of a deep vein thrombosis.Which of the following factors in this patient’s history most increased his riskfor developing this condition?
key_teaching_point: A prior episode of deep vein thrombosis significantly increases the risk of recurrence. Multiple coexisting factors (obesity, travel, sedentary work, medications) may contribute, but past DVT carries the greatest predictive value. Prompt anticoagulation is the cornerstone of treatment in patients with DVT.
explanation_summary: A prior history of DVT is one of the strongest predictors of recurrence. Patients who have had symptomatic DVT in the past have a 17–30% risk of developing another episode, especially if the initial cause was unprovoked or incompletely treated. Recurrence risk is highest within the first few years after the initial event, butpersists lifelong. Therefore, when evaluating a patient with a history of thrombosis and new symptoms, clinicians should maintain a high index of suspicion and consider prior DVT the most significant risk factor among others.DVT classically presents with unilateral leg swe

stem: A 21-year-old college football player presents to the clinic with right knee pain that started during a game two days ago. He was running toward the sideline when an opposing player collided with the inside of his right knee while his foot was planted. He immediately felt pain along the outside of his knee and had difficulty continuing to play. The pain has persisted since the injury, especially during walking or lateral movement. On physical examination, there is mild swelling and localized tenderness over the lateral aspect of the right knee. When the examiner applies gentle pressure outward on the medial side of the knee while stabilizing the lateral ankle, the right knee exhibits more lateral movement than the left. There is no bruising, deformity, or joint locking. Passive range of motion is preserved. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: LCL injury occurs after a varus stress to the knee, such as a blow to the medial aspect, often during sports trauma. explanation_summary: This young athlete presents with lateral knee pain following a direct blow to the medial side of the knee during a contact sport, with exam findings showing increased lateral joint movement when stress is applied. This mechanism and physical exam are classic for injury to the lateral collateral ligament (LCL). The LCL functions to stabilize the knee against varus stress — that is, forces that push the knee outward relative to the femur. stem: A 13-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to persistent discomfort in his right thigh and groin that has worsened over the past 3 weeks. He first noticed a dull ache after participating in a weekend soccer game, which he attributed to overexertion. The pain has not resolved with rest or over-the-counter analgesics, and he has been avoiding physical activity at school. His mother has noticed a progressive limp, particularly when he climbs stairs. He also mentions feeling more tired than usual and says he sometimes feels stiffness in the mornings, although this usually improves within an hour. His past medical history includes childhood eczema and mild asthma treated with inhaled corticosteroids. His diet is low in calcium-rich foods, and he spends most of his time indoors playing video games. There is no history of trauma, recent travel, rashes, or fever. His BMI is at the 97th percentile, and he is otherwise developmentally normal. On exam, he has a slightly externally rotated right lower extremity while standing. Gait assessment reveals limping and guarding on the right side. There is decreased range of motion at the right hip, especially with internal rotation. Knee and lumbar spine exams are unremarkable. There is no joint effusion or erythema. Neurologic exam is intact. A radiograph of the pelvis is shown below. Which of the following best describes the pathologic mechanism of this patient’s condition?
key_teaching_point: SCFE is the answer on USMLE for an overweight, preadolescent boy (10-13) with a painful limp. X-ray shows ice cream falling off the cone.
explanation_summary: The provided X-ray shows the right femoral head displaced posteriorly and inferiorly relative to the neck of the femur. This misalignment is consistent with slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) and classically described as the “ice cream slipping off the cone” appearance. SCFE is a disorder of the proximal femoral physis (growth plate) that typically affects preadolescent and early adolescent males between the ages of 10 and 14. During this period, rapid growth weakens the hypertrophic zone of the physis, making it susceptible to shear stress. In SCFE, the epiphysis remains in the acetabul

stem: A 16-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to persistent, intermittent left-sided chest pain for the past 4 days. The pain is described as sharp, non-radiating, and comes on suddenly at rest. He denies any cough, fever, palpitations, or shortness of breath. He reports that the pain does not change with food intake, but it worsens with certain upper body movements, including lifting his backpack or swinging a bat. He plays competitive baseball and had an intense upper body workout earlier this week. There is no history of recent viral illness or trauma. His medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any medications. On physical examination, he appears well and not in distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. Cardiovascular and pulmonary examinations are unremarkable. Palpation of the anterior chest wall reveals point tenderness over the left 2nd and 3rd costosternal junctions without swelling, erythema, or bruising. ECG and chest X-ray performed in the emergency department two days ago were normal. Laboratory tests including troponin, CBC, and CRP are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Costochondritis presents with localized, sharp chest pain worsened by movement and palpation. It is a benign, self-limited condition often seen in adolescents and athletes. Reproducibility of pain on exam helps distinguish it from cardiac, pulmonary, and GI causes. 26.1 A.Lateral meniscus tear B.Medial meniscus tear C.Pes anserine bursitis D.Posterior cruciate ligament tear E.Semimembranosus tendinopathy AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNoneTopic 27.1 A.Avoidance of high-impact physical activities B.Daily use of a calcium supplement C.Smoking cessation D.Vitamin D supplementation E.Weight loss PathologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Arthritis andspondyloarthropathies)SystemNoneTo
explanation_summary: Costochondritis is an inflammatory condition affecting the costosternal junctions, most commonly seen in adolescents and young adults. It presents with localized anterior chest pain that is sharp, non-radiating, and worsens with certain movements or palpation. It does not involve swelling and is usually self-limiting. The hallmark is reproducible pain over the costochondral or costosternal junctions. The condition often arises after repeated physical strain or minor trauma, such as vigorous upper body exercise, weightlifting, or repetitive movements in sports like baseball. There is typically

stem: A 33-year-old construction worker comes to the clinic due to pain in his left knee that started 2 weeks ago after he pivoted awkwardly while stepping off scaffolding. He says the pain is located on the inner side of the knee and occasionally feels like something is "stuck" when he tries to fully straighten it. He reports a few instances of a clicking sensation during extension while transitioning from a squatting to a standing position. He denies instability or swelling, but avoids kneeling or crouching due to discomfort. On examination, there is localized tenderness along the medial joint line, and when the leg is externally rotated during extension from a flexed position, he winces and describes a catching sensation. There is no visible effusion, and the remainder of the exam is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Medial meniscus tears cause medial knee pain with locking or catching, often after twisting trauma. Exam findings include medial joint line tenderness and pain/clicking with external rotation during extension. Diagnosis is clinical; confirmed by MRI, treated with conservative care or arthroscopy depending on severity.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with medial knee pain, mechanical symptoms of catching and locking, and pain with external rotation during knee extension, all of which are classic for a medial meniscal tear. The medial meniscus is more tightly attached to the joint capsule and is more frequently injured than the lateral meniscus. Twisting or pivoting movements, especially with a planted foot and flexed knee, are common mechanisms of injury in active individuals. Meniscal tears result in mechanical obstruction of motion within the joint. Patients typically report pain, locking, catching, or a clicking se

stem: A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with concerns about knee pain and stiffness that have developed gradually over the past 6 weeks. She describes aching in both knees, especially after a long day of teaching, and notes that the pain is worse when going up or down stairs. The stiffness tends to occur after prolonged sitting and improves somewhat with movement. She reports no redness, warmth, or swelling in the joints. Over the past year, she has gained about 9 kg (20 lb) due to emotional eating after her father’s passing. She admits to minimal exercise lately but had recently attempted to rejoin a weekend hiking group. After a few hikes, she found that her knee pain significantly worsened, which discouraged her from continuing. She has no history of joint disease, but her mother underwent knee replacement surgery in her 60s. She smokes about half a pack of cigarettes daily and has done so for nearly two decades. Her diet is irregular, and she often eats takeout or processed foods. She has no morning stiffness, no involvement of her hands or other joints, and no history of inflammatory disease. She takes no medications and has no allergies. On exam, her height is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) and weight is 91 kg (200 lb), giving her a BMI of 32.2 kg/m². Knee examination shows full range of motion, no effusion, and mild crepitus during flexion and extension. No tenderness is present over the joint lines or ligaments, and there is no erythema. Strength, sensation, and reflexes are intact in both lower extremities. The patient asks how she can reduce her risk of long-term joint problems. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation?
key_teaching_point: Obesity is the strongest modifiable risk factor for knee osteoarthritis. explanation_summary: Weight loss is the most effective intervention to reduce the risk of developing and worsening osteoarthritis in overweight or obese patients. Osteoarthritis is primarily a mechanical degenerative process that targets weight-bearing joints, and excess body weight substantially increases the load across the knees with every step. In fact, for every 1 kg (2.2 lb) of body weight lost, the load on the knee joint can be reduced by approximately 4 kg during daily activity. stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to a persistent limp and decreased activity over the past 6 weeks. His parents note that he used to be very active but now avoids running and complains of stiffness in the morning. There is no fever, rash, trauma, or recent illness. The child denies night sweats or weight loss. He is of normal weight and height and has met all developmental milestones. On physical exam, internal rotation and abduction of the right hip elicit discomfort. Gait assessment reveals mild antalgic walking and subtle pelvic drop on the left when standing on the right leg. There is no joint effusion, warmth, or swelling noted. A pelvic radiograph is obtained and shown below. Which of the following best explains this patient’s current condition?
key_teaching_point: Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease = idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head in boys aged 4–8. X-ray shows a “contracted” or flattened femoral head, not seen in SCFE or transient synovitis. Diagnosis is clinical + radiographic; early recognition prevents long-term joint deformity.
explanation_summary: This case is classic for Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease (LCP)—a form of idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head occurring in children between ages 4–8. The child presents with a gradual-onset limp, hip stiffness, and pain on passive range of motion, especially internal rotation and abduction. The lack of systemic signs (fever, rash, weight loss) argues against infectious or autoimmune etiologies. The radiograph is key in this diagnosis. stem: A 26-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic for evaluation of a swelling on the back of his right wrist, which he noticed about a month ago. He says it has remained painless and has not changed in size over the past two weeks. He works as a graphic designer and frequently uses a keyboard and mouse but denies any recent trauma, weakness, or numbness. He has no history of systemic disease, fever, or weight loss. He states that the mass is sometimes more prominent at the end of the day but does not interfere with daily activities. On physical examination, the wrist is non-tender with full active and passive range of motion. A 2-cm, soft, well- circumscribed swelling is noted on the dorsal aspect of the wrist, as seen in the image below. The mass is freely mobile under the skin and does not elicit pain on palpation. There is no overlying erythema, warmth, or fluctuation. Neurologic exam of the upper extremities is normal. Which of the following is the most likely outcome if this condition is left untreated?
key_teaching_point: Ganglion cysts are benign synovial fluid–filled swellings, most commonly found on the dorsal wrist. explanation_summary: This patient presents with a painless, mobile, well-circumscribed swelling on the dorsal wrist with no signs of inflammation, systemic illness, or neurological compromise. These features are characteristic of a ganglion cyst, a common benign lesion caused by the accumulation of gelatinous synovial fluid from adjacent joint or tendon sheaths. The most frequent location is the dorsum of the wrist, particularly over the scapholunate ligament. Ganglion cysts are typically asymptomatic and discovered incidentally, as in this case. stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with difficulty moving her right arm behind her back and discomfort in her shoulder for the past several weeks. She says the pain started gradually and is most noticeable when reaching to fasten her bra or retrieve items from her back pocket. She denies trauma, numbness, tingling, or prior shoulder surgery. The pain is dull, localized to the anterior shoulder, and not aggravated by overhead activity. It does not interfere with sleep. On physical examination, there is no swelling or asymmetry. Active range of motion is full in flexion and abduction. When asked to place the back of her hand against her lower back with her palm facing outward, she is unable to lift the hand away from her back. The movement reproduces her discomfort. The remainder of the shoulder exam is unremarkable. Which of the following muscles is most likely injured?
key_teaching_point: The subscapularis is the only rotator cuff muscle responsible for medial rotation and adduction of the shoulder. It is tested using the Gerber lift-off test, which isolates internal rotation strength. Inability to perform this maneuver suggests subscapularis tendon injury, even if other shoulder movements are preserved.
explanation_summary: This 40-year-old woman presents with progressive difficulty reaching behind her back and reproducing shoulder pain during internal rotation. The physical exam describes the Gerber lift-off test, where the patient places the dorsum of her hand on the lower back with the palm facing outward and attempts to lift it away from the body. Inability to do so —especially if painful—is a specific test for subscapularis pathology, which is responsible for medial rotation and adduction of the arm. The fact that abduction and flexion are preserved helps localize the weakness specifically to internal rotato

stem: A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic for evaluation of a bulge in his upper abdomen. He first noticed the swelling about a year ago when doing core exercises and says it has become more visible over time, especially when he tenses his abdominal muscles. He reports no pain, nausea, vomiting, or change in bowel habits. His medical history is unremarkable, and he has never had abdominal surgery. He takes no medications and denies weight loss or fevers. On physical examination, a small, firm, non-tender midline mass is palpated between the xiphoid process and the umbilicus. The mass becomes more prominent when the patient is asked to lift his head while lying supine and disappears at rest. There is no erythema, no overlying skin changes, and the rest of the abdominal exam is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Epigastric hernias occur in the midline above the umbilicus, through a defect in the linea alba. They are distinct from paraumbilical hernias based on location and are often reducible and asymptomatic. explanation_summary: This patient presents with a midline abdominal bulge located between the xiphoid process and the umbilicus, which is characteristic of an epigastric hernia. Epigastric hernias occur when small bowel or preperitoneal fat protrudes through a defect in the linea alba within the epigastrium, typically without a history of trauma or surgery. The defect allows intra-abdominal contents to herniate, forming a visible and sometimes palpable midline mass that becomes more prominent with increased intra-abdominal pressure (e.g., straining, lifting head from supine). stem: A 70-year-old man comes to the clinic because of progressive difficulty using his right hand. He says that over the past several years he has noticed increasing stiffness and trouble extending some of his fingers, which makes it harder to grasp objects and perform daily tasks. He denies any trauma, redness, or acute pain. His past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and chronic alcohol use. He is retired from construction work and spends most of his time at home. He reports no numbness or tingling but says that his hand sometimes feels “tight.” He notes the fingers never “snap back” into place once bent, and the stiffness remains constant rather than intermittent. Examination reveals palpable cords in the palm, and the hand lesion is demonstrated in the image. Sensation and pulses are intact. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation_summary: Progressively limited finger extension in an older man with intact sensation and pulses, long-standing diabetes, and chronic alcohol use, together with palpable cords in the palm and the lesion shown in the image, indicates Dupuytren contracture—a fibroproliferative disorder of the palmar fascia that produces flexion contractures of the ulnar digits. Clinical presentation is insidious, with stiffness, morning tightness, and functional difficulty placing the hand flat on a table (positive tabletop test). The deformity is constant, and unlike trigger finger, there is no intermittent snapping or

stem: A 76-year-old man comes to the clinic due to progressive difficulty hearing over the past year and a sensation of fullness in his right ear. He also reports that he recently had to purchase a larger hat because his old one felt tight. He denies ear pain, discharge, or prior surgeries. His past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and osteoarthritis. He takes metformin and acetaminophen as needed. On examination, there is bilateral frontal bossing and thickening of the skull bones. Neurologic exam reveals decreased hearing on the right but is otherwise normal. Laboratory evaluation shows normal serum calcium, phosphate, and parathyroid hormone levels. Serum alkaline phosphatase is markedly elevated. A lateral skull x- ray reveals thickened calvarium with areas of both lucency and sclerosis, producing a “cotton wool” appearance. The patient is otherwise asymptomatic and denies bone pain or recent fractures. His vitals are stable, and he has no focal tenderness or neurologic deficits. He is referred to endocrinology for further evaluation and counseling. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology of this patient’s condition?
key_teaching_point: Paget disease of bone is caused by excessive and disorganized bone remodeling driven by initial overactivity of osteoclasts followed by compensatory, chaotic osteoblast activity. This results in elevated ALP, normal serum calcium/phosphate/PTH, and a high bone turnover state with deformities, especially in the skull, spine, and pelvis.
explanation_summary: Paget disease of bone is a chronic skeletal disorder that arises from a defect in the normal process of bone remodeling. In healthy individuals, bone homeostasis is maintained through a coordinated balance between osteoclastic bone resorption and osteoblastic bone formation. In Paget disease, this tightly regulated process becomes disrupted, leading to a sequence of excessive osteoclastic resorption followed by an exaggerated but disorganized osteoblastic response. stem: A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of progressive rightknee pain that began over the past year. The pain worsens after long walks orclimbing stairs and improves with rest. He denies morning stiffness lastinglonger than 15 minutes and has no joint swelling or redness. He takes nomedications and has no significant past medical history. His BMI is 33. Physicalexam shows crepitus and tenderness along the medial joint line of the rightknee. His fingers show bony enlargement of the distal interphalangeal jointsbilaterally. X-rays of the knee reveal asymmetric joint space narrowing,subchondral sclerosis, and marginal osteophytes. Synovial fluid analysisreveals a white blood cell count of 900/µL and normal ESR. Which of thefollowing best explains the primary pathological process causing hissymptoms?
key_teaching_point: Osteoarthritis is a non-inflammatory joint disease caused by mechanical cartilage breakdown and subchondral bone remodeling, classically presenting with asymmetric joint involvement and low synovial WBC count. Inflammatory joint diseases show immune-mediated features and symmetric joint involvement.
explanation_summary: Osteoarthritis (OA) results from progressive wear-and-tear of articular cartilage leading to joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophyte formation. Loss of cartilage exposes the underlying subchondral bone, triggering remodeling and the appearance of "eburnation"—a polished ivory-like surface. This degeneration is mechanical and age-related, not immune-mediated. The synovial fluid in OA typically shows low WBC count (<2,000/µL), and ESR is usually normal,confirming a non-inflammatory process. Obesity and biomechanical stress accelerate cartilage breakdown and increase OA risk.

stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after injuring his right knee while playing in a local soccer match. He says he twisted his leg while rapidly pivoting to change direction and immediately felt a sudden popping sensation followed by severe pain and swelling. He was unable to continue playing and had difficulty walking off the field. He denies any recent trauma to the head or any similar prior injuries. On examination, the right knee is swollen with a moderate effusion. There is marked tenderness over the anterior knee. The Lachman test reveals increased anterior displacement of the tibia compared to the contralateral side. The patient has no signs of neurovascular compromise. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Anterior cruciate ligament tears typically follow a non-contact pivoting injury with a popping sound and rapid knee effusion. The Lachman test and anterior drawer test can be used for diagnosis. MRI confirms the diagnosis, and young athletes usually require surgical reconstruction with physical therapy. 36.1 A.Birth trauma B.Child abuse C.Osteogenesis imperfecta D.Physiologic bone fragility E.Vitamin D deficiency PathologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNoneTopic 37.1 A.Electromyography and nerve conduction studies and anti-Jo1antibodiesB.Lumbar puncture for albuminocytologic dissociationC.MRI for white matter plaquesD.Serum protein electrophoresisE.Sural nerve biopsy PathologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Muscular disorders)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: This young athlete presents with classic signs of an anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tear, including a non-contact twisting injury, an audible "pop", immediate pain and swelling, and difficulty bearing weight. These are hallmark clinical clues that strongly suggest ACL disruption, especially in high-demand sports such as soccer where rapid pivoting and deceleration are common injury mechanisms. The most telling physical finding in this case is the positive Lachman test, which is the most sensitive and specific physical examination maneuver for diagnosing ACL injury. stem: A 3-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department after his parents report that he rolled off a couch and fell approximately 40 cm (16 inches) onto a carpeted floor. They state that he cried briefly but then seemed “fine,” although over the past few hours he has refused to feed and keeps his left arm stiffly at his side. He was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. He is in the 30th percentile for weight, 40th percentile for length, and 50th percentile for head circumference. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals mild swelling and ecchymosis over the left upper arm, with decreased spontaneous movement of that extremity. No other bruising, oral injuries, or retinal findings are noted. A radiograph of the left humerus is shown. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this injury?
key_teaching_point: Spiral fractures are virtually always child abuse on USMLE. They occur due to rotational force applied to the bone.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with a fracture in a non-ambulatory infant, inconsistent with the reported mechanism of injury. Spiral fractures, particularly of long bones like the humerus, are essentially pathognomonic for child abuse because they require a rotational or torsional force that is unlikely to occur with a simple fall from a low height onto a carpeted surface. In infants younger than six months, accidental injuries are extremely uncommon due to limited mobility, and any fracture—especially with bruising—should raise concern for inflicted injury. stem: A 61-year-old man comes to the clinic due to a 2-month history of difficultyrising from chairs and climbing stairs. He reports no recent trauma but says hehas been feeling more fatigued and occasionally short of breath whenexerting himself. Medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus andhypertension, both well controlled. He does not use tobacco, alcohol, or illicitdrugs. Physical examination shows symmetric weakness in the deltoid, biceps,and quadriceps muscles bilaterally. There is no rash, sensory loss, or jointswelling. Deep tendon reflexes are preserved. Laboratory results show acreatine kinase level of 3,200 U/L (normal: 22–198), and erythrocytesedimentation rate is 72 mm/hr. Which of the following is the mostappropriate next step in diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: The USMLE wants electromyography and nerve conduction studies to diagnose polymyositis and dermatomyositis. Anti-Jo1 antibodies are also done. USMLE won't force you to choose between the two. 38.1 A.Ankylosing spondylitis B.Herniated lumbar disc C.Lumbar muscle strain D.Spondylolisthesis E.Vertebral osteomyelitis AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Arthritis andspondyloarthropathies)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: Polymyositis is due to a CD8+ infiltrate within the endomysium of proximal muscle fibers. It will present as proximal muscle weakness in a patient over 50, sometimes with pain and stiffness mimicking polymyalgia rheumatica. The difference, however, is that in polmyositis we have either 1) weakness on physical exam,and/or 2) increased serum CK. Neither of these are seen in PMR. Patients can complain of weakness, but if we do the physical exam and there's 5/5 strength in all extremities and CK isn't elevated, it's not polymyositis based on the physical exam alone.Diagnosis of polymyositis is don

stem: A 16-year-old boy comes to the clinic due to progressive lower back pain that has worsened over the past 2 months. He says the pain started gradually after he began participating in competitive gymnastics and has been bothering him mostly during extension-based activities such as jumping, backflips, and bridging movements. The pain is localized to the lower lumbar region and does not radiate down the legs. He denies any numbness, weakness, or bowel/bladder changes. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he is not taking any medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the lower lumbar spine. Neurologic examination is normal. While standing upright, the examiner notes a palpable indentation in the midline lower back and a visible prominence just above it. Straight-leg raise is negative. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Spondylolisthesis is forward displacement of a vertebral body, often due to pars interarticularis defects. Presents with mechanical back pain and may show a step-off or prominence on spinal palpation; confirmed by lateral spine X-ray. 39.1 A.Ewing sarcoma B.Langerhans cell histiocytosis C.Neuroblastoma metastasis D.Osteoid osteoma E.Osteomyelitis HistologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone tumors and tumor-likelesions)SystemNoneTopic 40.1 A.Anterior cruciate ligamentB.Fibular collateral ligamentC.Lateral meniscusD.Popliteus tendonE.Tibial plateau AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Miscellaneous)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: This adolescent male presents with gradually worsening low back pain exacerbated by hyperextension movements, in the absence of neurologic findings or systemic symptoms. His participation in high-impact sports like gymnastics is a key predisposing factor due to the repeated stress on the lumbar spine. The finding of a step-off on physical exam— described here as a prominence with an indentation below it—suggests vertebral displacement, characteristic of spondylolisthesis. stem: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother due to worsening right thigh pain for the past 3 weeks. The pain began insidiously and has become progressively more intense, especially at night. The child has been limping and recently refused to participate in sports activities. He reports occasional low- grade fevers and fatigue. There is no history of trauma or recent travel, but the patient had a mild viral illness 2 months ago. On physical examination, the right thigh is mildly swollen and tender to palpation over the midshaft of the femur, without overlying erythema or warmth. There is no joint effusion or lymphadenopathy. Laboratory results reveal a mild leukocytosis and elevated ESR. An X-ray of the right femur shows a poorly defined diaphyseal lesion with layered periosteal reaction. A biopsy is performed, and the histologic image is shown below. Based on this patient's presentation and histologic findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Ewing sarcoma is a diaphyseal malignant bone tumor of childhood that mimics osteomyelitis but has characteristic radiographic and histologic features. Biopsy shows small, round, uniform blue cells of neuroectodermal origin, and imaging reveals onion-skin periosteal reaction. Associated with the t(11;22) translocation and requires multimodal therapy for optimal outcomes.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with fever, localized bone pain, and radiographic findings of a diaphyseal lesion with periosteal reaction, which is a common presentation of Ewing sarcoma, a malignant bone tumor primarily affecting children and adolescents. The hallmark histologic appearance is closely packed, small round blue cells, seen in the image. These neoplastic cells are of neuroectodermal origin, and immunohistochemical staining may show CD99 positivity. The tumor is also associated with the t(11;22)(q24;q12) translocation, which results in an EWS-FLI1 fusion gene, a key diagnostic marker. The

stem: A 32-year-old man presents with vague lateral knee discomfort that worsens with prolonged standing and stair climbing. He reports intermittent instability when walking on uneven ground and difficulty maintaining balance while pivoting. He denies any history of trauma, swelling, or redness. His occupation requires frequent lifting, and he often stands with a wide-based stance. On examination, there is mild bowing of both legs with an increased intercondylar distance. The medial joint line is unremarkable, but tenderness is noted along the lateral aspect of the knee, just below the joint line. No effusion or warmth is appreciated. The varus stress test at 30° flexion elicits discomfort laterally without gapping medially. Gait reveals mild lateral deviation of the mechanical axis during stance phase. Which of the following structures is most likely under chronic strain in this patient?
key_teaching_point: Genu varum increases medial compartment compression and chronically stretches the fibular collateral ligament, a key lateral stabilizer resisting varus stress and external tibial rotation. 41.1 A.Axillary nerve compression affecting teres minor B.Bicipital tendinopathy limiting humeral stabilization C.Infraspinatus injury causing isolated external rotation deficit D.Labral tear producing mechanical instability E.Supraspinatus impingement causing abduction dysfunction AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Shoulder issues)SystemNoneTopic 42.1 A.Axillary nerve B.Median nerve C.Musculocutaneous nerve D.Radial nerve E.Ulnar nerve AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Upper limb nerves)SystemNoneTopic 43.1 A.Central nuclei in muscle fibers B.Endomysial inflammation with necrotic fibers C.Increased mitochondrial density in type I fibers D.Peripheral displacement of nuclei with ringed fibers E.Type I and II cells fiber type grouping HistologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Miscellaneous)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: The presence of genu varum (bow-legged alignment) in this patient leads to excessive mechanical loading of the medial knee compartment and stretching of the lateral stabilizers. The varus stress test at 30° flexion specifically isolates the fibular collateral ligament (FCL, or lateral collateral ligament), which becomes painful or tender when chronically strained. The lateral tenderness, absence of joint effusion, and preserved stability medially point toward repetitive traction on the fibular collateral ligament as the source of his pain and not meniscal or cruciate involvement.
