stem: A 35-year-old man comes to clinic complaining of ongoing weakness and discomfort in his right shoulder. He works as a professional camera operator and often holds heavy equipment close to his chest for long periods. Over the past month, he has noticed increasing difficulty rotating his arm outward and bringing it close to his body when repositioning gear. He denies trauma, dislocation, numbness, or tingling. He says he still manages his job but has become slower and avoids certain angles that aggravate his shoulder. On physical exam, there is no swelling, warmth, or discoloration. Strength and range of motion are preserved in shoulder flexion, extension, and abduction. When tested with the arm at the side, he demonstrates mild weakness during external rotation and reports discomfort when attempting to bring the arm back toward his torso. Neurovascular exam of the upper limb is normal. The empty-can and full-can tests are negative. Plain radiographs of the shoulder are unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient’s findings?
key_teaching_point: The teres minor, innervated by the axillary nerve, performs external rotation and adduction of the shoulder. If a patient has deficits in both external rotation and adduction, suspect teres minor, not infraspinatus.
explanation_summary: This 35-year-old man presents with isolated weakness in shoulder external rotation and adduction, without deficits in other planes of motion. His occupation involves prolonged isometric positioning and subtle shoulder movements, particularly with internal adjustments close to the body, which may lead to strain or nerve entrapment. The absence of trauma, numbness, or abduction weakness helps narrow the diagnosis. His physical exam is clean except for mild weakness during external rotation and adduction. The teres minor is a rotator cuff muscle responsible for external (lateral) rotation and add

stem: A 33-year-old man presents to the emergency department complaining of persistent right arm weakness that began two days ago after a minor trauma. He states that he recently returned from a 3-week trip to visit relatives in the Yukon territory of Canada, where he grew up. While there, he and his cousins spent several days hiking rugged trails, hunting, climbing through rocky outcrops, and paddling long distances on canoes and kayaks. He notes that during a climb near the cliffs bordering a river, he slipped and instinctively grabbed a branch to stop his fall, jerking his right arm forcefully. He felt a sudden pull and pain in the front of the arm, which he initially dismissed as a muscle strain. Later, while helping pack and lift furniture in preparation for the sale of his childhood home, he noticed increasing pain and weakness when bending his elbow or lifting with that arm. His symptoms worsened during the drive back into town, especially after prolonged gripping of the steering wheel. He denies any neck pain, paresthesia in the other arm, or systemic symptoms. He has no history of neurologic illness, trauma, or surgery. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and reports that he is normally very physically active. He has been back in town for 3 days and is concerned that the weakness is not improving. He says he cannot “curl” his arm at the gym, and when trying to bring food to his mouth with that arm, it feels heavy and uncoordinated. He also notes that turning a doorknob or twisting a screwdriver is difficult with that arm, though his hand seems to work fine otherwise. He has no problems with writing, buttoning a shirt, or holding tools, but says his right forearm feels “numb” when touched on the outer side. He does not recall dropping objects but says he hasn’t been using that arm much since the symptoms began. On exam, the right arm shows no visible deformities, abrasions, or ecchymoses. There is localized tenderness and mild swelling in the anterior upper arm. Motor testing shows marked weakness in elbow flexion and forearm supination. Wrist extension, finger flexion, and grip strength are intact. Shoulder abduction and internal/external rotation are preserved. Sensory exam reveals decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of the forearm, with intact sensation elsewhere. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric except for a slightly diminished biceps reflex on the right. X-ray of the arm shows no fractures or dislocations. Based on his exam, imaging, and mechanism of injury, the patient is diagnosed with a peripheral nerve injury. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured in this patient?
key_teaching_point: The musculocutaneous nerve innervates the elbow flexors and forearm supinators, and provides sensation to the lateral forearm. Injury presents with weakness in elbow flexion and supination and decreased lateral forearm sensation, often after stretch trauma to the anterior upper arm.
explanation_summary: The musculocutaneous nerve, originating from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus (C5–C7), innervates the anterior compartment of the arm—specifically the biceps brachii, brachialis, and coracobrachialis muscles—which are essential for elbow flexion and forearm supination. It also provides sensory innervation to the lateral forearm via the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm. This patient’s injury likely occurred during the forceful arm traction when he grabbed a branch to break his fall while climbing, a classic mechanism for stretch or traction injury to the musculocutaneous nerve. stem: A 44-year-old woman is referred to neurology for evaluation of persistent left posterior thigh weakness. Six months ago, she underwent surgery for a large posterior thigh liposarcoma that required careful dissection near the sciatic nerve. Following the procedure, she noticed difficulty extending her hip and flexing her knee. There was also mild atrophy of the left hamstring region. Over the following months, she participated in physical therapy and regained partial strength, but repeat electromyography was ordered to assess reinnervation progress. On neurologic examination, there is residual weakness in the left biceps femoris and semimembranosus muscles with noticeable thinning of the posterior thigh. Deep tendon reflexes are intact. Nerve conduction studies show evidence of reinnervation, and needle EMG of the left hamstring muscles reveals motor units that appear histologically homogeneous—containing clusters of similarly staining muscle fibers. These findings are consistent with chronic neurogenic remodeling following partial reinnervation. Which of the following histologic findings is most likely present in this patient’s recovering muscle?
key_teaching_point: Denervation followed by reinnervation leads to fiber type grouping, where same-type muscle fibers cluster together. This is a hallmark of chronic neurogenic injury and recovery, not seen in primary myopathies. EMG and histology findings together can help differentiate neurogenic from myopathic processes. 44.1 A.1B.2C.4D.6E.7 HistologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Miscellaneous)SystemNoneTopic 45.1 A.Branchial cleft cyst resulting from failed obliteration of embryonic cleft B.Congenital dermoid cyst arising from ectodermal entrapment C.Fibromatosis colli from birth-related injury to the sternocleidomastoid muscle D.Midline mass due to persistent thyroglossal duct remnant E.Reactive cervical lymphadenopathy secondary to recent viral infection AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Congenital and developmentalanomalies)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: This patient presents with weakness following surgical manipulation near the sciatic nerve, which innervates the hamstring muscles. The described symptoms—difficulty flexing the knee and extending the hip—are consistent with denervation of muscles such as the biceps femoris and semimembranosus. Over time, improvement in strength and EMG findings suggest partial reinnervation of these muscles, which helps us narrow down the underlying pathophysiology. During reinnervation, surviving motor neurons sprout collateral branches to reinnervate nearby denervated fibers. Importantly, the motor neuron d

stem: A 25-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive muscle fatigue and weakness over the past few months. He reports difficulty climbing stairs, rising from a seated position, and lifting objects above his head. His symptoms are symmetrical and more pronounced in the proximal muscles. He has no sensory deficits, no fasciculations, and deep tendon reflexes are preserved. Laboratory studies reveal mildly elevated creatine kinase levels, and electromyography demonstrates a myopathic pattern. Muscle biopsy shows fiber size variation and increased central nuclei. Electron microscopy reveals that certain regions of the sarcomere shorten during contraction, while others, specifically one band, maintain a constant length throughout. Which of the following labeled regions on the image remains unchanged in length during muscle contraction?
key_teaching_point: The A-band represents the full length of thick myosin filaments and does not change in length during contraction. The I-band (actin only), H-zone (myosin only), and sarcomere length all shorten during contraction.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with proximal muscle weakness, elevated CK, and a biopsy consistent with a congenital myopathy—findings that localize the problem to the muscle fiber and contractile apparatus. One of the diagnostic techniques used is electron microscopy, which allows visualization of sarcomere structure and its changes during contraction. Sarcomeres are the functional units of skeletal muscle and contain several key structural bands. During contraction, thin filaments (actin) slide past thick filaments (myosin), causing the sarcomere to shorten. However, not all bands change in size duri

stem: A 3-week-old newborn boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to a firm mass on the right side of his neck that they first noticed a few days ago. The baby was born at 39 weeks via forceps-assisted vaginal delivery after a prolonged second stage of labor. The pregnancy was uncomplicated, and the baby passed the newborn screening tests. The parents deny any recent fever, fussiness, feeding difficulties, or signs of infection. They state that the baby tends to keep his head turned slightly to the left, and they have noticed some asymmetry in how he holds his head when awake. On physical examination, a firm, non-tender 2-cm mass is palpable in the right lateral neck. The mass does not move with swallowing or tongue protrusion and there is no overlying skin change or drainage. Neurologic examination is normal, and the baby feeds and interacts appropriately for his age. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Fibromatosis colli is a benign neck mass in infants due to birth trauma to the sternocleidomastoid muscle. It presents as a firm, lateral mass in the neck within the first few weeks of life, often with associated torticollis. Diagnosis is clinical, and treatment is conservative with physical therapy. 46.1 A.AB.BC.CD.DE.EF.FG.GH.H AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNone 47.1 A.Ankylosing spondylitis B.Cervical spondylosis C.Cervical strain D.Herniated cervical disc E.Rheumatoid arthritis AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Spinal disorders and back pain)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: This infant presents with a firm, lateral neck mass associated with a history of forceps-assisted delivery and postural preference for head turning, which is classic for fibromatosis colli, a benign condition resulting from traumatic injury to the sternocleidomastoid (SCM) muscle during birth. Forceps or vacuum extraction, especially after a prolonged or difficult labor, can result in localized hemorrhage and fibrosis within the SCM. The firm mass reflects this fibrosis and typically appears within the first few weeks of life. Importantly, the mass is located laterally, is non-tender, and does

stem: A 16-year-old boy presents to the clinic after falling while skateboarding. He describes landing on his outstretched right hand and feeling immediate discomfort. Since the injury, he has had persistent pain in the central palm, with no numbness, tingling, or motor weakness. He denies any pain in the thumb or near the base of the thumb. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the central volar wrist, and no tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox. Range of motion of the wrist and thumb is mildly limited due to discomfort, but neurovascular examination is normal. Which of the labeled structures (A–H) is most likely fractured in this patient?
key_teaching_point: A FOOSH injury with central palmar pain and no snuffbox tenderness should raise suspicion for lunate fracture. Know the carpal bone anatomy in two rows: "She Looks Too Pretty; Try To Catch Her" — Scaphoid, Lunate, Triquetrum, Pisiform; Trapezium, Trapezoid, Capitate, Hamate. Lunate sits centrally in the proximal row and is commonly tested on USMLE in cases like this.
explanation_summary: This adolescent presents with wrist pain following a fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH), a common mechanism for carpal bone fractures. The key distinguishing feature in this case is pain localized to the central palm with no tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox, which effectively rules out scaphoid fracture. In contrast, central volar wrist pain is highly suggestive of injury to the lunate, the centrally positioned bone in the proximal carpal row. The lunate is vulnerable to axial compressive forces, and lunate fractures or dislocations can result from FOOSH injuries, especially when the wr

stem: A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic due to several months of neck pain that has gradually worsened. He describes the discomfort as a dull, aching sensation in the posterior neck that sometimes radiates to the shoulders, particularly after long periods of desk work or driving. He denies any trauma, recent infections, or constitutional symptoms. He also reports occasional stiffness in the morning lasting about 10–15 minutes but says it improves throughout the day. There is no history of numbness, weakness, or bowel/bladder dysfunction. He has well-controlled hypertension and takes lisinopril. Neurological examination is normal, with preserved strength and sensation in all extremities. Reflexes are symmetric. MRI of the cervical spine reveals degenerative changes involving the vertebral bodies and intervertebral discs, with osteophyte formation and mild narrowing of the neural foramina at multiple levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Cervical spondylosis is a common degenerative condition in older adults presenting with chronic neck pain. MRI findings often show disc space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and foraminal narrowing. explanation_summary: Cervical spondylosis refers to age-related degeneration of the cervical spine and is a common cause of chronic neck pain in older adults, particularly those over age 50. The condition involves disc degeneration, osteophyte formation, and sometimes narrowing of the neural foramina or spinal canal. This patient’s presentation of progressive, non-radiating neck pain with stiffness after inactivity and MRI showing degenerative vertebral changes strongly aligns with cervical spondylosis. Importantly, cervical spondylosis can present with or without neurologic findings. In this case, the absence of

stem: A 38-year-old man comes to the outpatient clinic with a 6-week history of right elbow discomfort that has gradually worsened. He works as a delivery driver. He says the pain is localized to the outer part of his elbow and is most noticeable when lifting boxes or turning doorknobs. The discomfort is particularly pronounced when he tries to extend his wrist while holding objects, such as a coffee mug. He denies numbness, tingling, or radiation of the pain down his arm. He has no prior joint disorders and takes no medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the lateral aspect of the right elbow. The pain is reproduced when the patient extends his wrist against resistance with the elbow fully extended. There is no swelling or erythema over the joint, and range of motion is preserved. The neurovascular exam of the right upper extremity is normal. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology in this patient’s condition?
key_teaching_point: Lateral epicondylitis causes pain at the lateral epicondyle, especially with resisted wrist extension. It results from microtrauma to the extensor carpi radialis brevis tendon. Treatment includes activity modification, NSAIDs, and use of a forearm counterforce strap.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with classic signs of lateral epicondylitis, also known as tennis elbow. He reports localized pain over the lateral elbow that is aggravated by activities involving wrist extension against resistance, such as lifting objects or turning doorknobs. The absence of neurologic symptoms or swelling helps rule out neuropathy or joint effusion. Lateral epicondylitis is caused by repetitive strain and microtrauma at the origin of the extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB) tendon, where it inserts at the lateral epicondyle of the humerus. stem: A 54-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute leftknee pain and swelling that began overnight. He reports two prior episodesaffecting the same knee over the last three years, both of which resolvedwithout intervention. His medical history is notable for hypertension and dailyalcohol consumption. On examination, the left knee is swollen, erythematous,warm, and tender with limited range of motion. Vital signs are within normallimits except for a temperature of 37.5°C (99.5°F). Synovial fluid is obtained foranalysis. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiologicmechanism responsible for this patient’s condition?
key_teaching_point: Gout is caused by monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints, leading to acute neutrophil-driven monoarthritis often triggered by metabolic risk factors. Diagnosis is confirmed by needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals, with treatment involving NSAIDs, corticosteroids, or colchicine acutely and xanthine oxidaseinhibitors chronically between flares.
explanation_summary: Gout is caused by supersaturation of uric acid in the serum, which leads to precipitation of monosodium urate crystals within synovial fluid. The classic site of involvement is the first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint, but other large joints such as the knee, ankle, and midfoot can also be affected. The disease occurs in flares marked by sudden-onset monoarthritis characterized by severe pain, erythema, swelling, and limited range of motion. Risk factors includechronic alcohol use, red meat or seafood-heavy diets, obesity, thiazide or loop diuretic use, and renal insufficiency. Importantly, h

stem: A 64-year-old woman presents to the clinic with gradually worsening kneepain over the past 6 months. The pain is worse with activity and improves withrest. She finds it difficult to get up after prolonged sitting and complains thather knees feel stiff in the morning but improve within 20 minutes. Her pastmedical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She has smokedone pack per day for the last 35 years and has a BMI of 33 kg/m². Onexamination, there is mild crepitus and tenderness along the medial joint lineof both knees but no erythema or effusion. Her gait is normal, and herneurological exam is unremarkable. Radiographs show joint space narrowingand osteophyte formation in the knees. The patient is interested in reducingthe long-term burden of her symptoms. Which of the following is the mosteffective intervention to reduce her risk of disease progression?
key_teaching_point: Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease driven by mechanical wear and tear. Obesity is the strongest modifiable risk factor, especially for weight-bearing joints. On USMLE, weight loss is the best preventive intervention, while acetaminophen is the first-line treatment for symptomatic OA. 51.1 A.Anterior hip dislocation B.Femoral neck fracture C.Intertrochanteric fracture D.Posterior hip dislocation E.Slipped capital femoral epiphysis AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNoneTopic 52.1 A.Anterior cruciate ligament tear B.Lateral meniscus tear C.Medial collateral ligament tear D.Patellar tendon rupture E.Posterior cruciate ligament tear AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNone
explanation_summary: Obesity is a major modifiable risk factor for the development and progression of osteoarthritis (OA), particularly in weight-bearing joints such as the knees. Losing as little as 5–10% of body weight has been shown to significantly reduce joint loading and inflammation, thereby decreasing symptoms and halting furthercartilage degradation. On USMLE, weight loss is the best long-term preventive strategy in overweight patients with OA. stem: A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after being involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He was the front-seat passenger and was not wearing a seatbelt. According to EMS, the car struck a guardrail and spun into a ditch. The patient was alert at the scene but complained of severe pain in the right hip and was unable to bear weight. He denies head trauma, loss of consciousness, or abdominal pain. There is no bleeding, open wound, or deformity of the thigh. On physical examination, the right leg appears shortened and held in a position of internal rotation at the hip. Passive range of motion is limited by pain. There is no sensory loss or distal neurovascular compromise. Radiographs are pending, but the orthopedic resident is consulted for evaluation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Posterior hip dislocation is the most common type and presents with a shortened, internally rotated leg. Often occurs in dashboard trauma scenarios, requiring prompt reduction to prevent avascular necrosis. USMLE tests leg position as a key diagnostic clue to distinguish dislocations and fractures.
explanation_summary: This patient presents after high-energy trauma with a shortened, internally rotated leg, which is the classic presentation of a posterior hip dislocation. This type of dislocation typically results from dashboard injuries, where a direct force is applied to the flexed knee, transmitting energy posteriorly through the femur to the hip joint. The femoral head is displaced posteriorly out of the acetabulum, causing the limb to assume a flexed, adducted, and internally rotated position. The physical findings of internal rotation and limb shortening are key distinguishing features of posterior hip

stem: A 26-year-old man presents to the emergency department after being involved in a motor vehicle collision. He was the restrained front-seat passenger in a car that struck a wall head-on. He recalls that his right knee hit the dashboard upon impact. He denies loss of consciousness but reports immediate pain and swelling in the right knee. On examination, there is a moderate joint effusion. The knee is not obviously deformed, and there is no visible bruising. When the patient lies supine with both hips and knees flexed to 90 degrees, the right tibia appears to sit farther back compared to the left. Gentle manipulation of the knee confirms increased backward movement of the tibia relative to the femur. The patient is otherwise hemodynamically stable and has no other injuries. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Posterior cruciate ligament injury is caused by a direct posterior force on the proximal tibia, such as hitting the dashboard in a car crash. Findings include posterior displacement of the tibia relative to the femur and increased posterior translation on exam. Diagnosis is clinical, supported by exam and mechanism, and confirmed with MRI. 53.1 A.Carpal tunnel syndrome B.Distal radius fracture C.Hook of hamate fracture D.Scaphoid fracture E.Triquetrum fracture AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNoneTopic 54.1 A.Anserine bursitis B.Cellulitis C.Patellar tendinopathy D.Prepatellar bursitis E.Septic arthritis PathophysiologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone/joint injuries andinfections)SystemNone
explanation_summary: This patient sustained a dashboard injury in a head-on collision in which his proximal tibia struck the dashboard while the knee was flexed, producing a force directed posteriorly along the tibia. This is the classic mechanism of a posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) injury, which stabilizes the tibia and prevents it from being displaced backward relative to the femur. The PCL is the strongest ligament in the knee, and it typically requires high-energy trauma to rupture. In this case, the patient has a posteriorly displaced tibia on inspection with the knees flexed, and there is increased poster

stem: A 25-year-old man comes to the emergency department due to pain in his right hand after falling off his bicycle earlier today. He was riding downhill at moderate speed when he lost control and landed on the handlebars with his body weight transmitted through the palm of his right hand. He did not hit his head or lose consciousness. He has no other complaints. On examination, there is localized tenderness over the hypothenar eminence. Grip strength is slightly reduced due to pain, and tapping over the affected region elicits tingling radiating to the ring and little fingers. There is no visible swelling, ecchymosis, or open wound. The patient is right-hand dominant and otherwise healthy. His wrist range of motion is intact, and capillary refill and radial pulses are normal. X-ray of the wrist is initially read as normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Hook of hamate fractures result from direct trauma to the palm (e.g., handlebar or racquet sports injury). Associated with ulnar nerve compression in the Guyon canal: look for paresthesia in the ring and little fingers. X-rays may be normal; diagnosis may require high suspicion and advanced imaging.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with hand pain localized to the hypothenar eminence after falling on the handlebar, with symptoms of ulnar nerve irritation (e.g., paresthesia of the fourth and fifth digits), which is classic for a hook of hamate fracture. These fractures typically occur when a fall or blunt force is transmitted through the palm, such as from gripping handlebars or sports equipment (e.g., baseball bat, tennis racket, or golf club). The hook of hamate lies on the ulnar side of the wrist and forms part of the Guyon canal, through which the ulnar nerve travels. Injury to this area can thus

stem: A 49-year-old man presents to the clinic with anterior knee pain and swelling that has developed gradually over the past week. He works as a plumber and spends much of his workday kneeling while installing fixtures. He says the knee feels sore and tight but not unstable, and he denies any trauma, locking, or popping. The pain is mostly noticeable when he kneels or presses directly on the front of his knee, and it does not limit his ability to walk or climb stairs. He denies fever, chills, or recent illness. He has no history of joint disease or gout. On physical examination, there is a soft, mildly tender swelling located over the front of the right knee, just above the patellar tendon. The overlying skin is intact with no erythema or warmth. Range of motion is full and non-painful. There is no joint effusion or ligamentous laxity. What is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Prepatellar bursitis presents with anterior knee pain and swelling in individuals with frequent kneeling or direct pressure on the patella. Physical exam shows localized anterior swelling with preserved range of motion and no signs of joint involvement. Diagnosis is clinical; treatment is conservative with NSAIDs and activity modification. 55.1 PathologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Miscellaneous)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: The diagnosis in this case is prepatellar bursitis, a condition characterized by inflammation of the bursa located between the patella and the overlying skin. This commonly occurs in individuals who kneel frequently, such as plumbers, tile workers, or gardeners, due to repetitive friction and pressure on the anterior knee. The patient in this vignette reports gradual-onset anterior knee pain with focal swelling, and he works in a kneeling-intensive occupation, making this diagnosis highly likely. On physical examination, the patient has localized swelling over the front of the knee that is sof

stem: A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to worsening pain in her right wrist for the past 6 days. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the outer (thumb- side) part of the wrist, and says it has made it increasingly difficult to carry out certain tasks like picking up her newborn daughter, who is 7 weeks old. The discomfort started mildly but has now progressed to the point where even turning a doorknob or lifting a coffee mug causes discomfort. She denies numbness, tingling, trauma, fever, or joint swelling. Her medical history is unremarkable. She is exclusively breastfeeding and has recently increased the frequency and duration of feedings. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the lateral (radial) wrist, just proximal to the base of the thumb. There is no visible swelling or redness, and full passive range of motion is preserved. However, the patient experiences a sudden sharp pain when asked to tuck her thumb into her palm, wrap her fingers around it, and bend her wrist toward the ulna. No other joint involvement is noted, and neurologic examination of the upper limb is normal. Which of the following best describes this patient’s condition?A. Entrapment of a peripheral nerve at the wrist B. Inflammation of tendon sheaths controlling thumb movement C. Joint capsule inflammation involving the radiocarpal articulation D. Postpartum overuse injury of a flexor muscle insertion E. Systemic inflammatory condition with asymmetric joint involvement
key_teaching_point: De Quervain tenosynovitis is caused by inflammation of the tendon sheaths of the first dorsal compartment, especially in postpartum women. It presents with radial wrist pain exacerbated by Finkelstein’s maneuver. 56.1 A.Calcium pyrophosphate crystal depositsB.Deposition of monosodium urate crystalsC.Focal loss of hyaline cartilage with subchondral bone thickeningD.Granulomatous inflammationE.Synovial pannus HistologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Arthritis andspondyloarthropathies)SystemNone
explanation_summary: This patient presents with localized lateral wrist pain in the context of repetitive hand use while breastfeeding, most notably worsened by the Finkelstein maneuver, which strongly suggests de Quervain tenosynovitis. The condition is characterized by inflammation of the tendon sheaths of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis muscles, which pass through the first dorsal compartment of the wrist. stem: A 33-year-old violinist comes to the clinic with progressive pain and stiffnessin her fingers, especially in the morning. Over the past 4 months, she hasfound it increasingly difficult to play her instrument due to finger jointdiscomfort. She reports swelling and warmth over several knuckles but deniesany recent trauma or fever. On physical examination, there is symmetricswelling, tenderness, and erythema of the proximal interphalangeal andmetacarpophalangeal joints. The distal interphalangeal joints are not affected.Hand radiographs show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. If asynovial biopsy were obtained, which of the following histopathologicfindings would most likely be seen?
key_teaching_point: Synovial pannus, a hyperplastic invasive tissue, is the hallmark histologic feature of rheumatoid arthritis and leads to progressive joint destruction. 57.1 A.Chondrosarcoma B.Ewing sarcoma C.Metastatic prostate cancer D.Osteoblastoma E.Osteosarcoma HistologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone tumors and tumor-likelesions)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: This patient has classic findings of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), characterized by prolonged morning stiffness and symmetric involvement of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints, with sparing of the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints. RA is a clinical diagnosis supported by serologies (positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies) and imaging findings such as joint space narrowing and marginal erosions, as seen in this patient. Synovial fluid analysis typically shows sterile inflammation (WBC 2,000–50,000/mm³, no crystals). Althou

stem: A 62-year-old man comes to the clinic due to persistent left hip pain that has gradually worsened over the past 5 months. He says the discomfort is dull and deep, often worse at night, and does not improve with over-the-counter pain medications. He denies recent trauma, fevers, or weight loss but says the pain is now making it difficult to sleep and walk longer distances. He works as a contractor and had been previously active, but the progressive pain has limited his ability to climb ladders. On physical examination, there is reduced range of motion of the left hip with deep palpation eliciting discomfort. Neurovascular exam is normal. Pelvic x-ray reveals a lytic lesion involving the left iliac bone with stippled calcifications. A core biopsy is performed, and a representative section is shown in the image below. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Chondrosarcoma is a malignant cartilage-forming tumor most common in older adults. Histology shows neoplastic chondrocytes embedded in lacunae in a cartilaginous matrix. Appears radiographically as lytic lesions with stippled or ring-like calcifications. The histo image is extremely buzzy for USMLE. 58.1 A.B lymphocytesB.GranulocytesC.Langerhans cellsD.MonocytesE.Plasma cells PathologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Bone tumors and tumor-likelesions)SystemNoneTopic
explanation_summary: This patient presents with slowly progressive deep bone pain, worse at night, and imaging findings consistent with a lytic, calcified lesion involving the pelvis. The histopathology reveals neoplastic chondrocytes embedded in lacunae, a classic appearance for chondrosarcoma — a malignant cartilage-forming tumor seen in older adults. The histo image is extremely buzzy for USMLE. It often arises in flat bones like the pelvis, femur, or proximal humerus and can appear radiographically as lytic lesions with “rings and arcs” or stippled calcifications, which represent mineralized cartilage. stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the clinic with a 3-month history of a painful,swollen skull lesion. Imaging reveals a well-circumscribed lytic lesion in theparietal bone. A biopsy of the lesion shows large, irregularly shaped cells withabundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and kidney-shaped nuclei. Electronmicroscopy of these cells reveals rod-shaped cytoplasmic inclusions. This patient’s condition is most likely caused by malignant proliferation of which of the following cell types?
key_teaching_point: LCH is the answer on USMLE for a patient with lytic lesions + Birbeck granules. The latter need not be tennis racquet-shaped and can be simply rod-shaped. CD1a positivity for LCH is buzzy. A Langerhans cell is a type of dendritic cell within the skin. explanation_summary: Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is a rare clonal proliferative disorder characterized by the accumulation of pathologic Langerhans cells, which are specialized dendritic cells normally involved in antigen presentation in the skin and mucosa.This condition typically affects children and can present with lytic bone lesions, such as in the skull, mandible, or long bones, leading to localized pain and swelling. stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to progressive limping and intermittent right hip pain for the past 3 weeks. His mother reports that he has been otherwise well, with no recent fever, trauma, or illness. The pain initially occurred only after prolonged running or playing but has now become more persistent. He has no complaints of pain in his knee or foot. The patient denies night sweats, weight loss, or malaise. He has no history of travel, tick bites, or recent infections. His past medical history is unremarkable, and his immunizations are up to date. He is not taking any medications. His academic and social development are age appropriate. There is no family history of rheumatologic or orthopedic conditions. On examination, he is afebrile and appears well. He walks with an antalgic gait, favoring the right side. There is no visible swelling, redness, or warmth of the hip. Passive internal rotation and abduction of the right hip are limited and mildly painful. The left hip and both knees are normal on examination. Neurovascular status is intact bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal normal ESR, CRP, and white blood cell count. Plain radiographs of the pelvis show increased density and slight flattening of the right femoral head, while the left femoral head appears normal. There is no joint space widening or signs of effusion. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease is idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head. It's the answer on USMLE for a hip issue in a kid age 5-8.
explanation_summary: Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease is a condition of idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head, most commonly seen in boys between ages 4 and 10 (but especially 5-8 range, as per my observation across NBMEs). It arises due to interruption of blood supply to the capital femoral epiphysis, which leads to necrosis, collapse, and gradual remodeling of the femoral head over time. The condition presents subacutely, often with a limp and mild hip or groin pain that progresses over weeks. stem: A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-month history ofgradually worsening stiffness in her fingers, difficulty opening jars, and apersistent dull ache in both wrists. She says the symptoms are worst in themorning and often make it hard to get dressed, though they improvesomewhat over the course of the day. She has also noticed that her weddingring feels tighter than usual, and she occasionally feels tingling in her palms.Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness at the second and thirdmetacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally, with restricted wrist flexion due todiscomfort. Tinel and Phalen maneuvers reproduce her paresthesias.Laboratory studies show a normocytic anemia and elevated C-reactiveprotein. Which of the following structures is most likely contributing to thenerve-related symptoms in this patient?
key_teaching_point: The transverse carpal ligament, aka flexor retinaculum, is the roof of the carpal tunnel and is the compressing structure in CTS.
explanation_summary: The transverse carpal ligament, aka flexor retinaculum, is the rigid roof of the carpal tunnel that is responsible for compression in carpal tunnel syndrome.The patient has rheumatoid arthritis, which can cause synovial inflammation and swelling within the carpal tunnel, compressing the median nerve. Normocytic anemia here is anemia of chronic disease.Tinel test is tapping over the median nerve at the carpal tunnel, which elicits paresthesias. stem: A 42-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 16-hour history of severe pain in his left first metatarsophalangeal joint. He has a past medical history of hypertension and obesity. He reports drinking several beers at a party the night before the pain started. Physical examination reveals a swollen, erythematous, and exquisitely tender big toe joint with markedly restricted range of motion. He is afebrile and denies any recent trauma or history of autoimmune disease. Laboratory studies are negative for rheumatoid factor and antinuclear antibodies. A joint aspirate is shown. What is the most immediate physiological mechanism responsible for the development of this patient's joint swelling?
key_teaching_point: Acute gout is caused by monosodium urate crystals triggering the innate immune response via NLRP3 inflammasome activation, leading to IL-1β release and increased vascular permeability. This allows neutrophil influx, causing joint swelling, pain, and erythema. Management includes NSAIDs, colchicine (within 36 hours), or corticosteroids if NSAIDs are contraindicated. explanation_summary: Increased vascular permeability is the key physiologic mechanism responsible for the rapid accumulation of neutrophils and subsequent swelling in acute gouty arthritis. Monosodium urate crystals deposited in the synovial fluid are recognized by the innate immune system, primarily through activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome in macrophages, which leads to the release of IL-1β. This cytokine, along with other pro-inflammatory mediators (e.g., TNF-α, IL-8), induces local endothelial cells to increase vascular permeability, allowing plasma proteins and neutrophils to exit the circulation and enter

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a six-month history of progressively worsening joint pain and stiffness. She describes the discomfort as a dull, aching sensation that began in her fingers and wrists and has now started affecting her ankles as well. The stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning and often lasts more than an hour before it gradually improves with activity. She says that on some mornings, she struggles to brush her teeth or hold a coffee mug due to pain and reduced grip strength. By the end of the day, the discomfort is milder, but it flares up again the following morning. She denies any recent trauma or overuse. In addition to the joint symptoms, she reports generalized fatigue and difficulty concentrating at work. She has also unintentionally lost around 3 kilograms over the past few months, despite no changes in her diet or exercise routine. She has no personal history of psoriasis, gastrointestinal issues, or genitourinary infections. She does not recall having a recent sore throat or viral illness. There is no family history of autoimmune disease, though her mother has osteoarthritis. She does not smoke or drink and works as a graphic designer. On further questioning, she denies any fever, photosensitivity, or oral ulcers. She has not had any miscarriages or issues with fertility. On physical examination, she appears tired but is in no acute distress. Her vital signs are normal. There is mild symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints and the proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her wrists are slightly puffy and tender to palpation with limited range of motion. There is no redness or warmth over the joints. Her distal interphalangeal joints are unaffected. Her ankles are also mildly swollen, with some tenderness on passive dorsiflexion, but no signs of enthesitis. There is no visible skin rash, no nail pitting, and no scalp lesions. The rest of her examination is unremarkable. Laboratory testing reveals anemia and an elevated inflammatory marker profile. Radiographs show soft tissue swelling and periarticular osteopenia without erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Rheumatoid arthritis presents in young women with chronic, symmetric small-joint inflammation, prolonged morning stiffness, positive RF and anti-CCP, and systemic features such as fatigue and weight loss. Early diagnosis is critical to prevent irreversible joint destruction and functional decline.
explanation_summary: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic autoimmune disease that primarily affects the synovial joints in a symmetric and progressive fashion. It most commonly presents in women aged 20–40 and is characterized by morning stiffness lasting more than one hour, symmetric small-joint involvement (especially the MCP, PIP, and wrists), and systemic symptoms such as fatigue, weight loss, and anemia. The disease results from chronic synovial inflammation leading to joint damage over time. Diagnostic testing often reveals elevated inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), positive rheumatoid factor (RF), and high

stem: A 28-year-old man comes to emergency after falling off his skateboard. Hehas pain in his left shoulder and holds his arm slightly abducted and externallyrotated. Physical exam shows decreased sensation over the lateral deltoid andweakness in shoulder abduction. An x-ray is performed. The left image showsa healthy patient. The right image shows our patient. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s acutepresentation?
key_teaching_point: Axillary nerve injury causes decreased sensation over the lateral deltoid and shoulder abduction. It can be caused by anterior shoulder dislocation or surgical neck of humerus fracture.
explanation_summary: The combo of loss of sensation over the lateral shoulder/deltoid and loss of shoulder abduction is buzzy for axillary nerve injury. We classically see axillary nerve injury on USMLE with surgical neck of humerus fracture, yes, but clearly in this x-ray we don’t have a fracture. Even if you have no idea what you’re looking at, thehumeral head is clearly displaced in the image.You could also know that an abducted + externally rotated extremity is seen with anterior dislocation of both the hip and shoulder. stem: A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with pain along the inner side of his right elbow that has gradually worsened over the past month. He works as a construction supervisor and often lifts heavy equipment and tools. He reports that the pain becomes more noticeable when he grips objects tightly or bends his wrist downward against resistance, particularly when using a hammer or wrench. He denies any trauma, numbness, or tingling in the hand or forearm. He has no prior joint problems and is otherwise healthy. Pain is elicited when the patient flexes his wrist against resistance with the elbow fully extended. No joint swelling, erythema, or warmth is noted, and neurologic examination of the right upper limb is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Medial epicondylitis involves overuse of wrist flexors and pronators, especially flexor carpi radialis and pronator teres. It presents with medial elbow pain exacerbated by resisted wrist flexion. explanation_summary: This patient presents with classic symptoms of medial epicondylitis, an overuse tendinopathy resulting from repetitive stress on the flexor-pronator muscle group of the forearm. His job involves gripping and repetitive wrist flexion, which can place strain on the flexor carpi radialis and pronator teres, the primary structures involved in this condition. The pain is localized to the medial epicondyle, which is the site of origin for these muscles, and is exacerbated by resisted wrist flexion, a hallmark of this diagnosis. stem: A 30-year-old man comes to the clinic due to persistent right knee pain that began after he twisted his knee while stepping off a curb three weeks ago. He says the pain is localized to the outer side of the knee and is aggravated by walking, especially when going up stairs or squatting. He also reports occasional episodes where the knee feels like it “gets stuck” while partially bent and then suddenly releases with a click. There is no history of swelling or instability, but he avoids certain positions because of fear the knee might catch again. On physical exam, there is mild tenderness along the lateral joint line. When the leg is internally rotated while extending the flexed knee, he winces and reports pain and a clicking sensation. The remainder of the exam is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Lateral meniscus tears present with lateral knee pain, often following a twisting injury, and produce mechanical symptoms like locking or catching. Exam may reveal joint line tenderness and pain/clicking with internal rotation and extension of the knee. Diagnosis is clinical, confirmed by MRI, and managed conservatively or with arthroscopy based on severity.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with lateral knee pain, a history of twisting injury, and symptoms of mechanical locking and catching, which are highly suggestive of a lateral meniscal tear. The menisci function as shock absorbers and stabilizers in the knee joint, and they are susceptible to injury during torsional stress when the foot is planted and the knee is flexed. Lateral meniscus tears are often less common than medial tears but tend to occur with rotational trauma, particularly in active individuals. The key symptom is intermittent locking or catching, especially when the knee is partially flex

stem: A 29-year-old man presents to the clinic due to persistent right shoulder weakness and discomfort. He works as a mechanic and first noticed difficulty reaching behind his head and rotating his arm outward when retrieving tools above shoulder level. He has no history of trauma, dislocation, or prior surgery. He says the symptoms began gradually over the past month and have worsened, particularly after prolonged use of pneumatic tools with overhead reaching. He has no neck pain, sensory changes, or systemic symptoms. His past medical history is unremarkable and he takes no medications. On physical examination, there is no visible deformity or swelling. Range of motion is preserved except for weakness and mild discomfort during resisted external rotation of the right shoulder with the arm at the side. Abduction and forward flexion are symmetric and painless. There is no scapular winging, and neurologic examination of the upper extremities reveals normal strength, reflexes, and sensation. The full-can and empty-can tests are negative. Radiographs of the shoulder are unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient’s weakness?
key_teaching_point: The infraspinatus muscle, innervated by the suprascapular nerve, is the primary rotator cuff muscle responsible for external rotation of the shoulder. Isolated weakness in external rotation without abduction issues suggests infraspinatus dysfunction, not supraspinatus. Suprascapular nerve injury can selectively affect infraspinatus with overuse or compression without involving the entire rotator cuff.
explanation_summary: This young patient presents with localized external rotation weakness of the shoulder, particularly provoked during resistance testing. His history of occupational overuse, especially with overhead and rotational tasks, increases the likelihood of chronic strain on the posterior rotator cuff muscles. Notably, he retains normal strength in abduction and flexion, which helps rule out supraspinatus pathology. These findings narrow the deficit to the muscles responsible for isolated external rotation—primarily the infraspinatus, and secondarily the teres minor. The infraspinatus muscle, innervated

stem: A 59-year-old woman comes to the clinic for evaluation of a bulge in her lower abdomen. She first noticed the swelling about six months ago, and it has gradually increased in size. The mass becomes more noticeable when she stands for prolonged periods or performs physical activity, and it tends to reduce or disappear when she lies down. She denies nausea, vomiting, constipation, or abdominal pain. Her medical history includes a laparotomy performed five years ago for small bowel obstruction, and she has not had any subsequent abdominal surgeries or trauma. On physical examination, there is a soft, non-tender, reducible mass located along the old surgical scar in the lower midline abdomen. The swelling becomes more prominent with Valsalva maneuver and is not associated with overlying erythema or warmth. No other abdominal or groin masses are palpated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Incisional hernias occur at the site of any prior abdominal incision, regardless of how long ago the surgery took place. explanation_summary: This patient presents with a reducible abdominal wall mass that occurs at the site of a prior abdominal incision, which is consistent with an incisional hernia. Incisional hernias develop at areas of prior surgical entry due to weakening of the abdominal wall fascia, leading to protrusion of intra-abdominal contents through the defect. These hernias can develop months or years after surgery, especially in patients with factors that impair wound healing (e.g., infection, obesity, increased intra-abdominal pressure), although no such risk factors are needed to make the diagnosis. stem: A 61-year-old woman presents to the clinic for evaluation of a bulge near her navel that she noticed about six months ago. She reports that it becomes more prominent when she coughs or strains, and it typically disappears when she lies flat. The swelling does not cause pain but has gradually increased in size. She denies nausea, vomiting, constipation, changes in appetite, or weight loss. Her medical history includes well- controlled type 2 diabetes and a prior appendectomy many years ago. She has no history of abdominal wall trauma or neonatal surgical conditions. On physical examination, there is a small, soft, non-tender midline bulge located just above the umbilicus. The mass is reducible and covered with normal-appearing skin. No other abdominal abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Paraumbilical hernias are skin-covered midline protrusions of bowel through the linea alba, just superior to the umbilicus. They differ from gastroschisis, which is congenital, uncovered, and located to the right of the umbilicus.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with a reducible midline abdominal bulge located just superior to the umbilicus, consistent with a paraumbilical hernia. A paraumbilical hernia occurs when small bowel herniates through a defect in the linea alba, the fibrous raphe running vertically down the midline of the abdomen. Unlike neonatal abdominal wall defects, a paraumbilical hernia is a skin-covered protrusion and is commonly seen in older adults, particularly women, often unrelated to any congenital anomalies. The fact that the hernia increases with straining and reduces when supine further supports its mech

stem: A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to numbness and tingling in her right hand for the past 3 months. The symptoms worsen at night and sometimes awaken her from sleep. She also reports difficulty opening jars and holding a coffee mug due to reduced grip strength. The patient works as a data analyst and spends several hours each day typing. On examination, sensation is decreased over the palmar aspect of the thumb, index, and middle fingers. There is atrophy of the muscles at the base of the thumb. Light tapping over the inner wrist reproduces her symptoms, and holding her wrists in forced flexion for 30 seconds also triggers the same discomfort. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured in this patient?
key_teaching_point: Compression of the median nerve within the carpal tunnel causes pain, paresthesia, and weakness in the median nerve distribution. Provocative testing and recognition of characteristic sensory and motor deficits are key to diagnosis.
explanation_summary: Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) is the most common entrapment neuropathy and results from compression of the median nerve as it passes through the carpal tunnel beneath the transverse carpal ligament. The median nerve provides motor innervation to the thenar muscles and lateral lumbricals, and sensory innervation to the palmar surface of the thumb, index finger, middle finger, and lateral half of the ring finger. CTS typically presents with paresthesia, nocturnal symptoms, hand weakness, and thenar muscle atrophy. Physical exam findings often include symptom reproduction when lightly percussing o

stem: A 13-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to right knee pain that has worsened over the past week. He plays as a forward on his school’s soccer team. He notes that the pain is exacerbated by sprinting, jumping, and climbing stairs. There is no history of recent trauma. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the tibial tuberosity and mild swelling just below the patella. The pain is reproducible with resisted knee extension. Radiographs of the knee show mild soft tissue swelling over the tibial tubercle with no bony abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Osgood-Schlatter disease is a common cause of anterior knee pain in adolescent athletes due to traction apophysitis of the tibial tubercle. explanation_summary: This patient presents with classic signs of Osgood-Schlatter disease (OSD), which is a traction apophysitis of the tibial tubercle—the insertion site of the patellar ligament. It commonly occurs in adolescent males undergoing rapid growth spurts, especially those active in running and jumping sports like soccer or basketball. During periods of growth, the quadriceps tendon pulls on the immature tibial tuberosity apophysis, resulting in microavulsions, pain, and inflammation. stem: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother due to progressive left hip and thigh pain for the past 3 weeks. He limps while walking and recently stopped participating in gym class. His mother says he has not had any recent trauma, fever, or known infections. The patient has no history of autoimmune or hematologic disease. He has no urinary symptoms, skin rashes, or weight loss. On physical exam, he walks with an antalgic gait, and passive internal rotation of the left hip elicits pain. His temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 90/min, and BMI is 22.4 kg/m². There is no swelling or erythema of the hip, but the range of motion is reduced. A hip radiograph is obtained and shown. Based on the clinical scenario and radiographic findings, which of the following best explains this patient's condition?
key_teaching_point: SCFE typically affects boys aged 10–14, with or without obesity, and presents with gradual-onset limp and limited hip motion. The hallmark radiographic sign is posteroinferior displacement of the femoral head relative to the neck — classically described as “ice cream slipping off the cone.” Prompt recognition and surgical pinning are essential to prevent complications such as avascular necrosis and early osteoarthritis.
explanation_summary: This 12-year-old boy presents with a chronic limp, groin/thigh pain, restricted internal hip rotation, and a classic radiograph demonstrating the femoral head displaced posteriorly and medially from the neck — the so-called “ice cream slipping off the cone” appearance. These findings are high-yield for slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE), a disorder of the growth plate (physis) common in boys aged 10–13. stem: A 45-year-old painter presents with 2 months of shoulder pain that worsenswhen lifting his arm overhead or reaching out to the side. The pain is localizedto the lateral shoulder and is most pronounced when he holds his arm out infront at shoulder height with the thumb pointed downward, resistingdownward pressure. He has noticeable weakness during this maneuver butmaintains full passive range of motion. There is no swelling, redness, orhistory of trauma. Strength is preserved in external and internal rotation.Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Supraspinatus injury is the answer if the patient has impaired shoulder abduction (especially the first 15 degrees) and has a positive empty-can, full-can, and/or drop-arm test. Shoulder pain while lying on one's side in bed or while reaching overhead is classic for both supraspinatus injury and subacromial bursitis. explanation_summary: Supraspinatus tendon injury is the most common rotator cuff strain/tear, typically presenting with lateral shoulder pain and weakness during the initiation of arm abduction (i.e., 0–15 degrees). The empty-can test involves forward flexing the arm to 90 degrees and internally rotating the thumb downward. If there is pain orweakness against resistance, this suggests supraspinatus injury. The full-can test, with the thumb pointed upward, can also detect injury and may elicit less discomfort while still revealing weakness. In more advanced cases, the drop-arm test is positive, where patients canno

stem: A 17-year-old boy presents to the clinic after injuring his wrist while skateboarding. He reports falling forward and landing on his outstretched right hand, using his palm to break the fall. Since the incident, he has experienced localized pain in the wrist, especially when attempting to grip or twist objects. He denies any numbness, tingling, or weakness in the hand. On examination, there is tenderness over the anatomical snuffbox on the dorsal-radial aspect of the wrist. There is no gross deformity, bruising, or swelling. Range of motion in the wrist is mildly limited due to pain, but he can fully move all fingers. A plain radiograph of the wrist is obtained and interpreted as normal, with labeled bones A through H shown below. Which of the following labeled structures is most likely fractured in this patient?
key_teaching_point: Scaphoid fractures are the most common carpal fractures and often present with anatomical snuffbox tenderness following a FOOSH injury. Initial radiographs may be normal; thumb-spica immobilization and follow-up imaging are essential to prevent avascular necrosis.
explanation_summary: This adolescent presents with anatomical snuffbox tenderness following a fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH) — a classic presentation for scaphoid fracture. The scaphoid is the most commonly fractured carpal bone and is particularly susceptible to injury due to its location and function as a bridge between the forearm and the hand. Importantly, early radiographs may appear normal in nondisplaced fractures. Therefore, a normal x-ray does not rule out a scaphoid fracture, especially when snuffbox tenderness is present. stem: A 27-year-old man comes to the physician with a 1-week history of rightshoulder pain after lifting weights. He reports discomfort specifically whenreaching overhead or rotating his arm outward. He denies numbness, tingling,or prior shoulder injuries. Physical examination reveals pain during lateralrotation of the shoulder. The Hornblower test is positive, eliciting weaknessand discomfort when attempting to externally rotate the arm while it’sabducted to 90 degrees. However, he is able to externally rotate the armwithout difficulty when his arm is at his side. Strength and sensation areotherwise normal. Which of the following injuries is most likely responsible forthis patient’s presentation?
key_teaching_point: Teres minor is the predominant lateral rotator of the arm when it’s fully abducted.
explanation_summary: Teres minor is the predominant lateral/external rotator of the arm when it is fully abducted (elbow up by your head). stem: A 2-year-old girl is brought to the clinic because she has refused to move her right arm since earlier this morning. Her father reports that the child was playing tug-of- war with her older brother, who yanked her by the right hand during the game. She immediately began crying and has not used the arm since. The parents became concerned when she refused to reach for her favorite toy or feed herself at breakfast, both of which she normally does with her right hand. She has no fever, has been otherwise active, and has not vomited or shown any signs of systemic illness. There is no history of trauma beyond the pulling incident, and the parents deny any falls, blunt injury, or previous problems with her joints. On examination, she appears calm but keeps the arm extended and pronated at her side. She cries when attempts are made to flex or supinate the elbow but does not resist shoulder motion. There is no visible swelling, bruising, or deformity of the upper extremity, and she exhibits no tenderness over the clavicle, humerus, or elbow. Passive range of motion at the shoulder is full and non-painful. The child continues to guard the arm and refuses to engage with toys when offered with the affected side. Vital signs are normal, and she is afebrile. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

explanation_summary: This child presents with a classic case of radial head subluxation, commonly known as nursemaid’s elbow. It typically occurs in children ages 1–5 when axial traction is applied to an extended, pronated forearm—such as when a child is pulled or lifted by the hand. The sudden force causes the annular ligament to slip over the radial head and become interposed between the radial head and the capitellum, preventing normal forearm motion. stem: A 43-year-old carpenter presents to the outpatient clinic due to left shoulder discomfort and difficulty lifting his arm. He reports that the pain began two weeks ago after he spent several hours sanding a ceiling, during which he repeatedly reached overhead. Since then, he has experienced pain when trying to lift objects away from his body and occasionally wakes up at night due to shoulder aching. He denies any numbness, tingling, or neck pain. He has no prior history of trauma or joint dislocation. On physical examination, he holds his left arm close to his body. Passive range of motion is preserved in all directions. However, when asked to actively abduct his left arm, he is only able to initiate a few degrees of movement before experiencing sharp pain over the lateral aspect of his shoulder. The pain worsens when resistance is applied while his arm is held at shoulder height in the “thumbs-down” position. No erythema, swelling, or warmth is noted around the shoulder joint. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's presentation?
key_teaching_point: The supraspinatus muscle, innervated by the suprascapular nerve, is responsible for the first 15° of arm abduction. Pain with resisted abduction, especially in the empty-can position, suggests supraspinatus tendonitis or injury. Passive range of motion is preserved in rotator cuff injury, distinguishing it from joint pathology.
explanation_summary: This middle-aged patient presents with classic symptoms of supraspinatus tendinopathy, a common overuse injury in individuals who perform frequent overhead arm movements, such as painters, athletes, or manual laborers. His symptom onset following ceiling sanding, nighttime shoulder pain, and difficulty initiating abduction are hallmark features of rotator cuff pathology. Importantly, passive range of motion is intact, indicating that the joint capsule and passive stabilizers are preserved, while active movement is impaired due to pain and dysfunction of the supraspinatus muscle. stem: A 68-year-old man comes to the clinic because of progressive difficulty walking and weakness in his arms and legs over the past two years. He reports that it began as a subtle heaviness in his thighs while climbing stairs but has gradually worsened. Now, he struggles to get up from low chairs and often drops objects when reaching overhead or gripping tools. He denies pain, numbness, or fasciculations. There is no history of trauma, infection, or new medications. He has well-controlled hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. On neurologic examination, he has mild atrophy of the quadriceps and forearm flexors. Strength testing reveals weakness in both proximal and distal muscle groups in the upper and lower extremities. Reflexes are normal and symmetric. Sensation to light touch, vibration, and proprioception is intact. Laboratory studies show normal creatine kinase, electrolytes, and thyroid function. An electromyogram is nonspecific, and the patient undergoes a muscle biopsy for further evaluation. Microscopic examination of muscle tissue reveals scattered basophilic vacuoles bordered by granular material within the myofibers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Inclusion body myositis is a slowly progressive inflammatory myopathy seen in patients over 50, often with both proximal and distal weakness. Basophilic rimmed vacuoles on biopsy are the diagnostic hallmark. It is often resistant to therapy, distinguishing it from other inflammatory myopathies like polymyositis or dermatomyositis. +
explanation_summary: This patient presents with a longstanding, slowly progressive muscle weakness involving both proximal and distal muscles in a man over the age of 50. These features strongly suggest inclusion body myositis (IBM), which is the most common inflammatory myopathy in older adults. Unlike other inflammatory myopathies that primarily involve proximal muscle groups, IBM uniquely affects both proximal (e.g., thighs, shoulders) and distal (e.g., wrist and finger flexors) muscles, often asymmetrically. The patient’s difficulty climbing stairs and gripping objects reflects this mixed pattern of weakness.

stem: A 14-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother due to right knee pain that has been gradually worsening over the past few weeks. He is otherwise healthy and plays on his school’s soccer team. The pain started after the beginning of the season and tends to worsen during practices, especially with sprinting or jumping. He denies any trauma, swelling, or instability of the joint. The pain improves with rest but recurs when he returns to activity. He reports no systemic symptoms, and the left knee is unaffected. On physical examination, there is focal tenderness and mild swelling just below the right patella. The knee joint is stable with full range of motion, and there is no redness or effusion. The remainder of the physical exam is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Osgood-Schlatter disease is caused by traction apophysitis of the tibial tuberosity in fast-growing adolescent athletes. Presents with anterior knee pain worsened by running or jumping, and tenderness below the patella. His symptoms are unilateral, localized just below the patella, and improve with rest. On examination, there is focal tenderness and swelling at the tibial tuberosity, without signs of joint effusion or instability. These features are characteristic of Osgood-Schlatter disease, a traction apophysitis of the tibial tubercle. stem: A 46-year-old right-handed woman presents to the clinic with progressive right shoulder pain for the past two months. She reports a dull ache over the lateral aspect of her shoulder that worsens at night, particularly when lying on the affected side. The pain is aggravated by reaching overhead or attempting to put away items on high shelves. She denies any recent trauma, weakness, numbness, or systemic symptoms. She works as a receptionist and has no history of autoimmune or endocrine disorders. Physical examination reveals localized tenderness over the anterior and lateral shoulder with a painful arc between 60° and 120° of active arm elevation. Passive range of motion is preserved in all directions. Strength is normal in elbow flexion, wrist extension, and grip. A plain radiograph of the right shoulder is obtained and shown. Further examination is most likely to reveal impairment of which of the following active shoulder movements?
key_teaching_point: Calcific tendinitis of the supraspinatus tendon causes pain during active shoulder abduction due to inflammation and impingement. The supraspinatus initiates the first 15° of abduction, followed by the deltoid and then scapular rotators. Radiographs often show calcium deposits near the greater tuberosity.
explanation_summary: This patient presents with chronic shoulder pain that worsens when lying on the affected side and during overhead activities. The physical exam shows a painful arc of movement and localized tenderness over the anterior and lateral shoulder. The plain radiograph reveals a radiopaque deposit (as indicated by the arrow) in the region of the supraspinatus tendon—findings consistent with calcific tendinitis of the shoulder. stem: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her babysitter because she suddenly stopped using her right arm after being pulled forcefully while resisting getting into her car seat. According to the babysitter, the child cried briefly, then became calm but has since refused to move the arm. The girl is alert and cooperative but holds the right upper limb extended and pronated by her side. She resists passive supination of the forearm and flexion of the elbow. There is no swelling, bruising, erythema, or deformity noted. Range of motion at the shoulder is preserved, and there is no tenderness over the clavicle or humerus. She is afebrile and otherwise well-appearing. What is the most likely diagnosis?
key_teaching_point: Radial head subluxation (nursemaid’s elbow) presents in toddlers with sudden refusal to use the arm held in a pronated, extended position after axial traction; diagnosis is clinical, and immediate reduction (via supination/flexion or hyperpronation) typically restores function. 81.1 A.Age at last menstrual periodB.Caffeine consumptionC.Dietary historyD.Past use of combined oral contraceptivesE.Sleep qualityF.Family history of autoimmune disease PhysiologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Metabolic bone disorders)SystemNone
explanation_summary: Radial head subluxation, or nursemaid’s elbow, is a common orthopedic injury in children between ages 1 and 5, resulting from axial traction on an extended, pronated forearm. This typically occurs when a caregiver pulls, lifts, or swings a child by the hand or forearm. The mechanism leads to the annular ligament slipping over and becoming trapped between the radial head and the capitellum, preventing normal rotation of the forearm. stem: A 52-year-old woman comes to the office for a wellness visit. She isperimenopausal and has no history of fractures or chronic illness. Her motherwas diagnosed with osteoporosis in her mid-60s and sustained multiple wristand spine fractures. The patient works as a school administrator and reportsgoing to the gym two to three times a week for light cardio and strengthtraining. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and is not takingany prescription medications. Her weight is 49 kg (108 lb), and her height is162 cm (5'4"), with a BMI of 19. She asks whether she is at increased risk forbone loss and if further evaluation is needed. Which of the followingadditional information would be most helpful in assessing this patient's riskfor osteoporosis?
key_teaching_point: Routine weight-bearing exercise, adequate dietary intake of calcium and vitamin D, and BMI >19 all help decrease the risk of osteoporosis.
explanation_summary: This patient has two key non-modifiable risk factors: a maternal history of osteoporosis and a low body weight. She maintains a healthy lifestyle, including regular exercise and avoidance of tobacco, excessive alcohol, and medications known to affect bone health, all of which are protective. The most relevant missing detailis her intake of calcium and vitamin D, which are critical for maintaining bone mass.
